cards.title: Ahab and Starbuck
cards.summary: Starbuck is Ahab's most sustained moral counterforce, but he cannot finally redirect the captain's will.
cards.student_note: Their scenes are strongest when read together: conscience keeps speaking, yet command and obsession keep tightening.

cards.title: Ahab's Biblical Name
cards.summary: Ahab's name carries biblical associations with corrupt kingship, idolatry, and destructive authority.
cards.student_note: Students do not need a full Bible background to notice the effect: Melville gives his captain a name that already sounds like a warning about rule and obsession.

cards.title: Ahab's Command Performance
cards.summary: Ahab often commands by staging scenes that make others participate in his obsession.
cards.student_note: The Quarterdeck is the clearest example, but later chapters keep showing command as theater, ritual, and pressure.

cards.title: Cetology and Classification
cards.summary: The cetology chapters classify whales, but they also test the limits of classification itself.
cards.student_note: These chapters can feel like interruptions. They matter because Ishmael wants knowledge to be orderly, while whales and language keep exceeding his categories.

cards.title: Cetology And Modern Science
cards.summary: Melville's whale science is historically important but not the same as modern marine biology.
cards.student_note: Students can respect the chapter's ambition while noticing where nineteenth-century classification differs from current science.

cards.title: Coffin and Life-Buoy
cards.summary: Queequeg's coffin changes meanings: death object, crafted possession, ship equipment, and survival device.
cards.student_note: The symbol works because it does not stop being a coffin when it becomes useful. Survival depends on an object marked by death.

cards.title: Coffin Survival Arc
cards.summary: Queequeg's coffin links illness, craft, ship safety, and Ishmael's survival.
cards.student_note: The object matters because its meaning changes without losing its association with death.

cards.title: Comedy Under Pressure
cards.summary: Comic scenes often arrive near danger, not outside it.
cards.student_note: The jokes show how Ishmael and the crew live with fear, monotony, and violence.

cards.title: Extracts As Archive
cards.summary: The extracts make the whale a problem collected across cultures, genres, and centuries.
cards.student_note: The prefatory collage is not ordinary plot. It shows that the whale arrives already surrounded by books, myths, jokes, sermons, and reports.

cards.title: Fast-Fish, Loose-Fish, and Property
cards.summary: Ishmael turns a whaling rule about possession into a broader meditation on law, empire, and who gets to claim what.
cards.student_note: This is one of Melville's clearest methods: begin with a technical whaling custom, then let it expand into politics and moral argument.

cards.title: Fedallah And Prophecy
cards.summary: Fedallah's presence makes prophecy feel both theatrical and structurally important.
cards.student_note: The guide should separate the novel's ominous design from its racialized description of Fedallah.

cards.title: Forecastle Polyphony
cards.summary: The forecastle chapters let many sailors' voices interrupt the captain-centered plot.
cards.student_note: The Pequod is a mixed global crew, not only Ahab's private instrument. These scenes keep that wider human world audible.

cards.title: Gams and News at Sea
cards.summary: A gam is a meeting between whaling ships, often for news, mail, supplies, stories, and warnings.
cards.student_note: The gams keep reminding students that the Pequod is part of a wider whaling world, even as Ahab narrows every encounter to Moby Dick.

cards.title: Hawthorne Dedication Context
cards.summary: The dedication places Melville's novel near Hawthorne's dark symbolic fiction.
cards.student_note: Students do not need a full biography, but the dedication signals ambition beyond adventure narrative.

cards.title: Inns, Chapels, Thresholds
cards.summary: Before the Pequod sails, public rooms train readers to notice strangers, rituals, warnings, and grief.
cards.student_note: The shore chapters build the social and religious pressures that the voyage carries out to sea.

cards.title: Jonah and Father Mapple's Sermon
cards.summary: Father Mapple retells Jonah as a story about fleeing duty, facing judgment, and speaking truth.
cards.student_note: The sermon acts as a warning system for the voyage. The hard question is who recognizes the warning and who ignores it.

cards.title: Late Mercy Tests
cards.summary: Late encounters repeatedly give Ahab chances to choose human claims over the chase.
cards.student_note: The ending is built from refusals, not simple fate. Several chapters show Ahab meeting reasons to stop and pressing on.

cards.title: Lookout And Perception
cards.summary: Masthead and lookout scenes turn seeing into work, temptation, and risk.
cards.student_note: Watching the sea becomes practical labor and also a metaphor for interpretation.

cards.title: Navigation Versus Obsession
cards.summary: Ahab's navigation scenes show technical command being bent toward one destructive aim.
cards.student_note: In these chapters, instruments and seamanship still work, but Ahab narrows their purpose until skill becomes another servant of obsession.

cards.title: Pip, Castaway, and Mental Strain
cards.summary: Pip's abandonment at sea changes how the novel speaks about fear, survival, and sanity.
cards.student_note: This material needs careful classroom handling. The novel's language can be startling, but Pip is not just comic relief; he becomes one of the book's sharpest truth-tellers.

cards.title: Pip, Trauma, Witness
cards.summary: Pip's abandonment changes him and makes him a painful witness to the voyage's moral cost.
cards.student_note: Handle these chapters carefully: the text uses older language for madness, but the scene is also about terror, isolation, and survival.

cards.title: Property And Empire Thread
cards.summary: Whaling property rules expand into questions about political power and imperial claim.
cards.student_note: Fast-Fish and Loose-Fish is the center, but the thread reaches earlier labor and later legal satire.

cards.title: Queequeg And Religious Difference
cards.summary: Queequeg's scenes mix friendship with Christian judgments about non-Christian practice.
cards.student_note: The guide should help students see both Ishmael's growing affection and the prejudiced vocabulary that frames it.

cards.title: Race, Empire, and the Global Crew
cards.summary: The Pequod's crew is international and multiracial, but the narration often describes people through nineteenth-century stereotypes and imperial assumptions.
cards.student_note: A student edition should neither erase the harm in the language nor flatten the book into a single attitude. The guide should name the assumptions, explain the context, and let students see where the novel challenges or repeats them.

cards.title: Religious Difference and Harmful Labels
cards.summary: The novel often describes non-Christian religion through comic, Christian, and racialized assumptions.
cards.student_note: The guide should help students see both the warmth of Ishmael's friendship with Queequeg and the harm in labels such as heathen, idolator, and savage.

cards.title: Ship Meetings As Warning System
cards.summary: The gams form a network of news, rumor, loss, and refusal.
cards.student_note: Each meeting asks whether Ahab can still hear anything except news of Moby Dick.

cards.title: Shipboard Hierarchy
cards.summary: The Pequod runs through ranks: captain, mates, harpooneers, specialists, and ordinary sailors.
cards.student_note: That hierarchy matters because Ahab's private obsession can move the whole ship only because command structure already exists.

cards.title: Shore To Sea
cards.summary: The early chapters move Ishmael from city mood to shipboard commitment.
cards.student_note: Use this card to track how the novel slowly converts private restlessness into a public voyage.

cards.title: Sperm Whale Anatomy
cards.summary: Melville's sperm-whale chapters focus on head, spout, skin, tail, skeleton, and oil-bearing parts.
cards.student_note: The anatomy chapters are not only trivia. They show how work, profit, awe, and incomplete science all meet in the body of the whale.

cards.title: Storm, Fire, and Navigation
cards.summary: The late storm chapters turn ordinary navigation tools into symbols of authority, rebellion, and broken guidance.
cards.student_note: Compass, quadrant, lightning, and log-line are practical tools first. Their symbolic force comes from watching them fail or get remade under Ahab's pressure.

cards.title: The Pequod As A System
cards.summary: The ship gathers owners, officers, harpooneers, sailors, profit, discipline, and obsession into one moving world.
cards.student_note: Job titles and routines matter because they explain how Ahab's will travels through an institution.

cards.title: The Three-Day Chase
cards.summary: The ending unfolds over three chase days, with pursuit becoming ritual escalation.
cards.student_note: Each day increases damage, narrows choices, and confirms Ahab's isolation from the people around him.

cards.title: Final Chase Escalation
cards.summary: The final chase tightens over three chapters, making each refusal more costly than the last.
cards.student_note: Track how each day gives Ahab another chance to treat disaster as warning, and how each chance is refused.

cards.title: The Whale Body As Text
cards.summary: Ishmael repeatedly treats the whale's body as something to read, classify, and misread.
cards.student_note: This connects anatomy chapters to the novel's larger worry about interpretation.

cards.title: The Whale Knowledge Problem
cards.summary: Ishmael keeps asking what kind of knowledge can describe a whale: science, story, picture, labor, or myth.
cards.student_note: When chapters feel encyclopedic, students can ask what each method reveals and what it fails to hold.

cards.title: Tools And Small Chapters
cards.summary: Several short chapters isolate a tool, action, or technique so whaling can be seen as skilled work.
cards.student_note: The scale is deliberate: tiny objects can hold the whole industry together.

cards.title: Violence After The Hunt
cards.summary: The killing of a whale is followed by more bodily labor, danger, and extraction.
cards.student_note: Whaling violence does not end when the whale dies; the aftermath remains part of the work.

cards.title: Whaling as Labor
cards.summary: The Pequod is not only an adventure setting; it is a workplace organized around danger, hierarchy, bodily skill, and profit.
cards.student_note: When the book pauses for ropes, blubber, try-works, or shipboard routines, it is often showing the labor system that makes Ahab's quest possible.

cards.title: Whiteness And Reading Risk
cards.summary: The whiteness chapter collects meanings until the symbol becomes unstable and disturbing.
cards.student_note: Readers should resist a single fixed meaning. Melville makes whiteness powerful partly by letting meanings clash.

cards.title: Whiteness as a Symbol
cards.summary: The whiteness chapter gathers many meanings without resolving them into one stable symbol.
cards.student_note: Students should resist asking for a single answer. Melville makes whiteness frightening partly because it can mean too much and too little at once.

cards.title: Opening Water Meditation
cards.summary: Loomings links mood, city life, money, death-thoughts, and water before the plot begins.
cards.student_note: The famous first sentence opens into emotional diagnosis: Ishmael goes to sea because staying ashore has become dangerous to his mind.

cards.title: Boarding-House Comedy
cards.summary: The early inn chapters use comedy to make fear, class, race, and intimacy visible.
cards.student_note: The jokes lower Ishmael's guard before the friendship with Queequeg becomes serious.

cards.title: Religion Before The Voyage
cards.summary: The chapel, pulpit, and sermon frame the voyage with grief, judgment, duty, and sacred warning.
cards.student_note: The religious shore chapters matter because the voyage carries their warnings into Ahab's command world.

cards.title: Friendship And Contract
cards.summary: Ishmael and Queequeg's bond becomes emotional, domestic, religious, and practical.
cards.student_note: The friendship teaches Ishmael a different kind of loyalty before the ship's hierarchy takes over.

cards.title: Nantucket Mythmaking
cards.summary: The Nantucket chapters turn a real whaling port into legend.
cards.student_note: The humor and exaggeration show how economic history can become national myth.

cards.title: Owners And Risk
cards.summary: Peleg and Bildad turn whaling danger into contracts, shares, investment, and moral language.
cards.student_note: Before Ahab appears, the novel shows that whaling is already a business structure that can make danger sound ordinary.

cards.title: Prophecy On The Dock
cards.summary: Warnings cluster around the Pequod before departure.
cards.student_note: The warnings do not stop the voyage; they teach readers to carry dread forward.

cards.title: Ahab's Delayed Entrance
cards.summary: Ahab is talked about long before he fully appears.
cards.student_note: Delay turns Ahab into a pressure before he becomes a person on deck.

cards.title: Ship As Workplace
cards.summary: The Pequod runs through ranks, watches, shares, and routines as much as through symbolism.
cards.student_note: Shipboard structure helps explain how one captain's purpose can move many different workers toward the same danger.

cards.title: Quarterdeck As Stage
cards.summary: Ahab turns command into public theater.
cards.student_note: The scene works like drama: speech, props, chorus, and ritual all help bind the crew to one desire.

cards.title: Soliloquy Sequence
cards.summary: Chapters 37 through 39 let Ahab, Starbuck, and Stubb speak in theatrical isolation.
cards.student_note: The short chapters reveal conflicts that ordinary narration might hide.

cards.title: White Whale Before Whiteness
cards.summary: The book first gives Moby Dick a history, then turns whiteness into a philosophical problem.
cards.student_note: Separating these chapters helps readers distinguish rumor about a whale from meditation on a symbol.

cards.title: Chart And Control
cards.summary: Ahab's chart joins knowledge, calculation, and obsession.
cards.student_note: The chart is a real tool, but the chapter asks whether Ahab's tools are serving judgment or desire.

cards.title: Affidavit And Belief
cards.summary: The Affidavit defends the story's plausibility by piling up witness-like testimony and named examples.
cards.student_note: The chapter matters because a wild story suddenly wants to sound evidentiary, almost legal.

cards.title: First Lowering Chaos
cards.summary: The first lowering turns anticipation into weather, speed, confusion, and bodily risk.
cards.student_note: After many preparatory chapters, whaling becomes immediate action. The danger is no longer theoretical.

cards.title: Fedallah And Fear
cards.summary: Fedallah is surrounded by prophecy, racialized description, and crew suspicion.
cards.student_note: The guide should help students notice both the plot function and the stereotype pressure around him.

cards.title: Gam Network
cards.summary: Ship meetings create a network of stories, warnings, and missed obligations.
cards.student_note: Each gam tests whether Ahab can listen to anyone else's story.

cards.title: Pictures And Misreading
cards.summary: Whale pictures become a way to study error, distance, and representation.
cards.student_note: The visual chapters show how hard it is to know a whale from images alone.

cards.title: The Line And Danger
cards.summary: The whale-line is useful, deadly, and hard to escape.
cards.student_note: The tool becomes a symbol because it literally binds workers to danger.

cards.title: Stubb's Comedy And Violence
cards.summary: Stubb's humor often appears beside brutality.
cards.student_note: Students should notice how laughter can make violence easier to watch and harder to judge.

cards.title: Processing The Whale
cards.summary: The middle labor chapters show whale hunting as industrial extraction.
cards.student_note: These chapters answer the practical question of what happens after a whale is killed.

cards.title: Heads And Knowledge
cards.summary: The head chapters turn anatomy into interpretation.
cards.student_note: Ishmael keeps treating the whale's body as a text, but the body resists being read completely.

cards.title: Monkey-Rope Dependence
cards.summary: The monkey-rope makes human dependence literal by tying Ishmael's safety to Queequeg's dangerous work.
cards.student_note: The scene turns friendship and labor into shared bodily risk, one of the novel's clearest images of interdependence.

cards.title: Grand Armada Contrast
cards.summary: The Grand Armada holds violence, panic, tenderness, and stillness inside the same whale herd.
cards.student_note: The calm center complicates simple adventure reading: whaling can look thrilling, brutal, beautiful, and vulnerable at once.

cards.title: Law Of Fish
cards.summary: Fast-Fish and Loose-Fish turns whaling law into political satire.
cards.student_note: The legal joke widens into questions about property, empire, and power.

cards.title: Pip And Trauma
cards.summary: Pip's abandonment changes his role in the novel.
cards.student_note: The guide should treat Pip's scenes with care: the book's older language can be harsh, but his insight becomes central.

cards.title: Try-Works At Night
cards.summary: The try-works scene combines labor, fire, temptation, and spiritual disorientation.
cards.student_note: This chapter is a strong checkpoint for how the voyage changes perception itself.

cards.title: Coffin Transformations
cards.summary: Queequeg's coffin changes meaning several times before the end.
cards.student_note: Tracking the coffin helps students see how objects carry memory across chapters.

cards.title: Tools Fail
cards.summary: Late navigation tools break, burn, or are rejected.
cards.student_note: The failure of tools makes Ahab's command feel increasingly cut off from ordinary seamanship.

cards.title: Storm And Command
cards.summary: The storm chapters test Ahab's authority against fear, electricity, and crew resistance.
cards.student_note: Storm scenes make command physical. Weather, tools, and frightened sailors all press against Ahab's effort to control meaning.

cards.title: Rachel's Refusal Test
cards.summary: The Rachel asks Ahab to interrupt the chase for another family's loss.
cards.student_note: Ahab's refusal makes the final ethical stakes painfully clear.

cards.title: The Symphony Before The Chase
cards.summary: The Symphony gives Ahab one last chance to imagine another relation to home, love, and Starbuck.
cards.student_note: The chapter matters because it lets readers feel the road not taken.

cards.title: Three-Day Chase Structure
cards.summary: The final chase uses repetition, escalation, and narrowing choice.
cards.student_note: Read the three chapters as one structure: damage accumulates, warnings fail, and alternatives disappear.

cards.title: Epilogue Survival
cards.summary: The epilogue explains Ishmael's survival through aftermath, rescue, and biblical echo.
cards.student_note: The ending makes survival feel both accidental and charged with the duty to tell.

cards.title: Right Whales, Brit, and Feeding
cards.summary: The brit chapters show a different whale ecology from the sperm-whale hunt that drives Ahab.
cards.student_note: These chapters help students see that Melville's whales are not interchangeable. Feeding habits, body parts, and value shape how crews look at each species.

cards.title: False Sightings and Desire
cards.summary: Spirit-Spout, Squid, and The Hat turn seeing into suspense, mistake, omen, and desire.
cards.student_note: The crew often sees through expectation. Ahab's hunt changes how ordinary sights are interpreted.

cards.title: Eating the Whale
cards.summary: The whale-as-food chapter turns industrial whaling into questions of appetite, custom, disgust, and use.
cards.student_note: The novel keeps asking what it means to turn an enormous living body into products, meals, and jokes.

cards.title: Right-Whale Head Superstition
cards.summary: The crew kills a right whale partly because Stubb and Flask believe its head will balance or protect the ship.
cards.student_note: The chapter mixes practical seamanship, superstition, and casual violence. It is a strong place to ask how work habits become beliefs.

cards.title: Sperm-Whale Head as Weapon
cards.summary: The Battering-Ram chapter explains why the sperm whale's head can be imagined as a force of impact.
cards.student_note: This card connects anatomy to plot: Ishmael's technical description prepares readers to believe that the whale's body can damage ships.

cards.title: Case, Junk, and Spermaceti
cards.summary: Several head chapters distinguish the sperm whale's oil-bearing parts and the labor of extracting them.
cards.student_note: When vocabulary gets dense, keep the core idea in view: the whale's head is being translated into valuable substances through dangerous work.

cards.title: Whale Social Groups
cards.summary: Schools, bulls, cows, and calves give Ishmael a way to describe whale society, even when his language carries nineteenth-century assumptions.
cards.student_note: Students should notice both the observation and the metaphor. Ishmael describes whale groups through human social language, which can reveal and distort at the same time.

cards.title: Body Parts and Sacred Language
cards.summary: The Cassock uses religious language for whale anatomy and processing work.
cards.student_note: The shock comes from the mixture: sacred vocabulary is attached to bloody industrial labor.

cards.title: Whaling Houses and Global Industry
cards.summary: The Decanter links the Samuel Enderby to the history and geography of British and American sperm whaling.
cards.student_note: Names, dates, ports, and firms widen the voyage into an industry, so Ahab's hunt moves through global commercial history.

cards.title: Craftsmen Around Ahab
cards.summary: The carpenter and blacksmith chapters show practical workers drawn into Ahab's symbolic mission.
cards.student_note: These chapters are not only comic side scenes. They show how skilled labor gets bent toward Ahab's obsession.

cards.title: Calm Before Destruction
cards.summary: Late quiet chapters make beauty feel temporary and unstable before the final chase closes in.
cards.student_note: The pauses matter because they offer emotional alternatives to Ahab's pursuit, even as the plot keeps moving toward destruction.

cards.title: Pip and Ahab Below Deck
cards.summary: Pip's loyalty and Ahab's damaged tenderness come into conflict with the hunt below deck.
cards.student_note: Pip reaches a side of Ahab that almost looks humanly responsive, but the pursuit still wins.

cards.title: Final Gams as Ethical Tests
cards.summary: The late ship meetings test whether Ahab can respond to another crew's suffering.
cards.student_note: The Rachel and the Delight make refusal visible. Ahab keeps choosing the chase over urgent human claims.

cards.title: Ambergris Trade
cards.summary: Ambergris turns the whale body into perfume, medicine, commerce, comedy, and natural-history uncertainty.
cards.student_note: This card gives students the commodity frame for a chapter that can otherwise feel like a strange digression.

cards.title: Arsacides Skeleton Display
cards.summary: The Arsacides chapters use a displayed whale skeleton to test what measurement and spectacle can explain.
cards.student_note: The skeleton gives Ishmael facts and scale, but it also proves how much of the living whale remains beyond display.

cards.title: Fossil Whales and Deep Time
cards.summary: The fossil-whale chapters expand the book's scale from shipboard labor to geology, extinction, and ancient life.
cards.student_note: They matter because they make the whale older than the voyage and larger than any single hunt.

cards.title: Ahab's Leg
cards.summary: Ahab's ivory leg links injury, command, repair, dependence, and symbolic force.
cards.student_note: The leg is not only a physical detail. It keeps Ahab's wound visible while showing how much his authority relies on other people's labor.

cards.title: The Carpenter's Bench
cards.summary: The carpenter's workspace gathers repair, measurement, tools, comedy, and bodily improvisation.
cards.student_note: The scene reads better when students connect the jokes to real shipboard labor and to Ahab's dependence on practical craft.

cards.title: Potters' Fields
cards.summary: The Pacific's calm includes burial-ground imagery, so the quiet sea is also a field of death.
cards.student_note: This card helps students see why The Pacific is ominous instead of simply peaceful.

cards.title: Perth the Blacksmith
cards.summary: Perth's craft skill, ruined body, and grief make him part of the book's labor system, not just a background worker.
cards.student_note: Ahab needs Perth's work, but the chapter also gives Perth a history of loss. The forge scene turns private pain into shipboard labor.

cards.title: The Gilder
cards.summary: The title names the sea's gilded surface, where beauty, softness, and danger coexist.
cards.student_note: This quiet chapter belongs in the chase sequence because its beauty is unstable; it offers calm without safety.

cards.title: The Quadrant
cards.summary: The quadrant is a navigation instrument Ahab destroys when it no longer answers the question he cares about.
cards.student_note: Students need the tool explained so the act reads as both practical sabotage and symbolic rage.

cards.title: Corpusants
cards.summary: The storm-fire phenomenon becomes weather, omen, spectacle, and command test at once.
cards.student_note: Knowing the concrete phenomenon helps students understand why the crew reads the scene as supernatural pressure.

cards.title: The Compass Needle
cards.summary: The compass needle is a guidance tool that Ahab forces back into service after the storm disrupts it.
cards.student_note: The chapter has a technical surface and a symbolic center: Ahab refuses even broken guidance unless he can dominate it.

cards.title: Coffin-Life-Buoy
cards.summary: Queequeg's coffin becomes a life-buoy, turning a death object into a rescue object before the ending.
cards.student_note: The transformation matters because it lets the book's darkest object carry Ishmael into survival.

cards.title: Like Cures Like
cards.summary: Ahab frames Pip as a strange medicine for his own disorder, even as the hunt pulls him away.
cards.student_note: The phrase helps students read the scene as emotional logic, not just odd wording. Pip's care threatens Ahab's purpose because it reaches him.

cards.title: The Delight
cards.summary: The ship's name is grimly ironic: the Delight brings another warning and another image of wreckage.
cards.student_note: The gam lands harder when students see the name's irony. Ahab treats warning as confirmation rather than interruption.

cards.title: Communal Eating and Early Fellowship
cards.summary: Breakfast and Chowder use shared meals to move Ishmael from isolation toward shipboard society.
cards.student_note: Food scenes become social tests, showing how strangers become companions before the voyage turns them into a crew.

cards.title: New Bedford Street as Global Port
cards.summary: The Street turns New Bedford into a compact view of global whaling money, labor, fashion, and movement.
cards.student_note: Before the Pequod appears, the town already shows the wider economy that will make the voyage possible.

cards.title: Queequeg Before the Pequod
cards.summary: Biographical and Wheelbarrow give Queequeg a past, a comic social presence, and a practical path toward the ship.
cards.student_note: These chapters keep Queequeg from being only Ishmael's first impression; he has history, skill, humor, and agency.

cards.title: Departure Work Rhythm
cards.summary: All Astir and The Mat-Maker show the voyage forming through preparation, weaving, and ordinary work.
cards.student_note: The plot advances through labor before it advances through chase. Routine work keeps gathering pressure around Ahab's unseen purpose.

cards.title: Bulkington and the Lee Shore
cards.summary: Bulkington's brief return lets Ishmael imagine truth as risk, exposure, and refusal of safe shore life.
cards.student_note: The chapter is short but important because it turns a minor sailor into an emblem of dangerous independence.

cards.title: Postscript and Whaling Prestige
cards.summary: The Postscript adds comic evidence to Ishmael's defense of whaling dignity.
cards.student_note: The small chapter shows Ishmael still arguing for whaling's cultural importance after The Advocate.

cards.title: Ahab and Stubb's First Conflict
cards.summary: Enter Ahab; To Him, Stubb stages command as a clash between ordinary sailor speech and Ahab's absolutism.
cards.student_note: Stubb jokes, but Ahab makes even joking dangerous. The chapter rehearses later conflicts over obedience.

cards.title: Surmise and Interpretive Pressure
cards.summary: Surmises shows Ishmael building explanations from partial evidence, rumor, and pressure.
cards.student_note: The chapter reminds readers that the narrator often has to infer motives rather than simply report them.

cards.title: Town-Ho as Nested Story
cards.summary: The Town-Ho's Story turns shipboard violence into a nested tale told through several layers of audience and memory.
cards.student_note: The frame matters because Melville makes storytelling itself part of the evidence, suspense, and uncertainty.

cards.title: Sphynx and the Unreadable Body
cards.summary: Ahab questions the whale's severed head as if it could reveal hidden knowledge.
cards.student_note: The scene makes the whale body feel like an unreadable oracle: present, enormous, and silent.

cards.title: The Virgin and Whaling Competition
cards.summary: The Jungfrau gam turns whaling into competition, embarrassment, and economic pressure between ships.
cards.student_note: This meeting shows the Pequod inside a working industry where reputation and success matter as much as danger.

cards.title: Cleanup After Extraction
cards.summary: Stowing Down and Clearing Up turns the whale's body into stored oil, then restores the ship's temporary order.
cards.student_note: The clean deck does not erase the violence of extraction. It only prepares the ship to repeat the cycle.

cards.title: The Bachelor as False Ending
cards.summary: The Bachelor offers cheer, profit, and homeward motion just as Ahab presses deeper into refusal.
cards.student_note: The meeting matters because another ending is visible: success, return, and fellowship. The Pequod cannot take it.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Extracts
cards.summary: Extracts opens with a chain of borrowed whale references that frames the novel as a text built from citations and catalogues.
cards.student_note: Notice how the front matter already works like a mini-archive. It previews the book's habit of mixing quotation, natural history, and whaling lore before the story begins.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: The Counterpane
cards.summary: Ishmael wakes with Queequeg's arm over him and starts rethinking his fear of the harpooneer.
cards.student_note: The chapter turns a startling bedside scene into a quick change of judgment. Ishmael notices the patchwork quilt, the tattooed arm, and the tomahawk, then reads Queequeg differently.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Breakfast
cards.summary: At breakfast, Queequeg's calm table manners and the sailors' awkward silence sharpen Ishmael's comic observations.
cards.student_note: Pay attention to the contrast between the rough seamen and their social shyness. The scene also keeps Queequeg readable as both strange and surprisingly composed.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: The Street
cards.summary: Ishmael's walk turns New Bedford into a whaling town shaped by docks, foreign sailors, and sea money.
cards.student_note: The chapter expands the setting beyond the inn. Melville links the town's wealth and architecture to the ocean labor that brought both people and profit there.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Nightgown
cards.summary: Ishmael and Queequeg linger in bed, smoke together, and move into a late-night story about Queequeg's past.
cards.student_note: The chapter slows the plot so friendship can settle in. Shared warmth, shared smoke, and the shift into Queequeg's history make their bond feel earned.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Biographical
cards.summary: Ishmael sketches Queequeg's island home, royal family, and reasons for choosing a seafaring life.
cards.student_note: This is a selective portrait rather than a full biography. It explains why Queequeg leaves home, how he meets Christianity, and why he keeps returning to sea.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Wheelbarrow
cards.summary: The wheelbarrow episode follows Ishmael and Queequeg to the waterfront while exposing how often other people misread them.
cards.student_note: The chapter mixes comic travel detail with a rescue at sea. It shows Queequeg as capable, quick, and repeatedly underestimated.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: Nantucket
cards.summary: Nantucket is presented as an island whose identity, economy, and imagination are all bound to the sea.
cards.student_note: Melville makes the island feel almost entirely maritime. Even its landscape and history are described through whaling and ocean travel.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Chowder
cards.summary: At the Try Pots, the clam-or-cod joke leads to a memorable meal and a warning about harpoons in the bedroom.
cards.student_note: The chapter stays comic, but the inn's coffin-and-gallows imagery keeps danger close. Hospitality and foreshadowing share the same table.

cards.title: Scripture, proof, and authority: The Prophet
cards.summary: Elijah's warnings make the Pequod feel haunted before Ishmael and Queequeg ever sail.
cards.student_note: The chapter matters because its prophecy is indirect and unsettling. Elijah never fully explains himself, so suspicion becomes part of the voyage's atmosphere.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: All Astir
cards.summary: The Pequod's outfitting fills the chapter with supplies, spare gear, and the labor of getting ready to sail.
cards.student_note: This is a logistics chapter in the best Melville sense. The inventory of provisions and replacements shows how costly and fragile a whaling voyage is.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Merry Christmas
cards.summary: Peleg and Bildad get the Pequod under way on Christmas Day, mixing prayer, scolding, and seamanship.
cards.student_note: Watch the way humor and piety overlap here. The departure also underlines how much of the ship's authority still sits with the owners, not Ahab.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: The Lee Shore
cards.summary: Bulkington becomes a brief emblem of the sailor who chooses open sea peril over safe landfall.
cards.student_note: Melville uses him to argue that some forms of truth and freedom belong to the sea rather than to comfortable shore life.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: Postscript
cards.summary: Ishmael adds one last defense of whaling by linking whale oil to royal ceremony.
cards.student_note: The postscript is comic but purposeful: it tries to dignify whaling by imagining kings and queens touched by the same substance the ship hunts.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Enter Ahab; To Him, Stubb
cards.summary: Ahab finally appears on deck, and his sharp exchange with Stubb leaves the mate rattled.
cards.student_note: The chapter is really about the atmosphere Ahab creates. Even a short encounter shows how his presence warps the crew's sense of what is normal.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: The Pipe
cards.summary: Ahab tries to smoke, finds the pipe useless, and throws it overboard.
cards.student_note: The small action matters because it rejects ordinary comfort. Ahab's obsession is already too tight for a simple pipe to soothe him.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Queen Mab
cards.summary: Stubb retells a dream about Ahab's ivory leg and turns it into a joke that still feels threatening.
cards.student_note: The dream keeps returning to Ahab's body and authority. Even in comic form, it suggests that the captain's leg is tied to bigger danger.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Cabin-Table
cards.summary: The dinner table becomes a map of shipboard rank, with Ahab ruling the officers and the harpooneers eating more freely below.
cards.student_note: Melville uses the meal to show how authority works in practice. Who sits where, who speaks, and who is served all matter.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: The Masthead
cards.summary: The masthead turns lookout duty into a meditation on dreaming, danger, and losing oneself in the sea.
cards.student_note: The chapter is funny about bad lookouts, but serious about attention. A sailor's mind can drift just when the ship most needs him alert.

cards.title: Theatrical form: Sunset
cards.summary: In a stage-like soliloquy, Ahab speaks of his burden, his pain, and the fixed purpose that drives him.
cards.student_note: The form matters: Ahab sounds like a tragic actor talking to himself, and the speech lets readers hear the tension between his glamour and his ruin.

cards.title: Theatrical form: Dusk
cards.summary: Starbuck voices his fear of Ahab's quest while the ship's revelry leaves him feeling divided between duty and pity.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives Starbuck a moral burden, not a clean heroic stance. He can see the danger, but obedience and compassion both hold him fast.

cards.title: Theatrical form: First Night-Watch
cards.summary: Stubb jokes his way through the watch, treating Ahab's danger and the night itself with uneasy laughter.
cards.student_note: His humor looks light, but it is also a way of surviving uncertainty. The stage form keeps the watch feeling open and vulnerable.

cards.title: Theatrical form: Hark!
cards.summary: Hark! builds suspense from whispers, small sounds, and the suspicion of hidden people below deck.
cards.student_note: The chapter is brief because the uncertainty is the point. The ship begins to feel secretive before the crew understands why.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: The Chart
cards.summary: Ahab studies sea charts in private, narrowing the voyage to a hunt for Moby Dick.
cards.student_note: The chart matters because it shows Ahab turning ordinary navigation into a secret pursuit. Watch how the captain reads the sea as a path toward one prey.

cards.title: Scripture, proof, and authority: The Affidavit
cards.summary: Ishmael gathers testimony and examples to argue that Moby Dick's violence should be believed.
cards.student_note: The chapter is less neutral report than legal brief. Its evidence matters, but so does Ishmael's urgent need to persuade readers that the whale is not a fantasy.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Surmises
cards.summary: Melville pauses to consider whether Ahab can still behave like a working captain while chasing Moby Dick.
cards.student_note: The chapter lives in the gap between business and obsession: the ship still has to fish, even when the true goal is personal revenge.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: The Mat-Maker
cards.summary: A quiet afternoon at the mat turns into a moment of premonition before the first lowering.
cards.student_note: The sword-mat scene matters because the calm feels temporary; ordinary deck work is already shadowed by the danger to come.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The First Lowering
cards.summary: The first lowering turns Ahab's hidden crew and the whale hunt's danger into open action.
cards.student_note: This chapter changes the book's speed. The boats go down, Fedallah appears, and the chase becomes physical risk rather than only rumor or command.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: The Hyena
cards.summary: Ishmael turns his bad luck into a joke about himself after the failed lowering.
cards.student_note: The hyena image lets him name fear without hiding it: he feels foolish, unlucky, and exposed at once.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: The Town-Ho ’s Story
cards.summary: The Town-Ho's story becomes a nested tale of mutiny, punishment, prophecy, and Moby Dick.
cards.student_note: The chapter is long because rumor travels through many mouths. Track who tells the story, who hears it, and how each layer affects belief.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: Of the Less Erroneous Pictures of Whales, and the True Pictures of Whaling Scenes
cards.summary: Ishmael sorts bad and better whale pictures to ask what accurate representation can look like.
cards.student_note: The chapter is not only about art. It teaches students to ask how people know whales when most images of them are partial, stylized, or wrong.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: Of Whales in Paint; in Teeth; in Wood; in Sheet-Iron; in Stone; in Mountains; in Stars
cards.summary: Melville moves from one whale image to another to show how badly people represent what they claim to know.
cards.student_note: Each example measures the distance between the whale itself and the human versions made in art, tools, and display.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: Brit
cards.summary: The Pequod crosses brit fields and sees right whales feeding in their natural ground.
cards.student_note: The calm, wide scene slows the chase and shows the whale as part of a food web, not just a target.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: Squid
cards.summary: A strange squid-like sighting leaves the ship in suspense and deepens the sense that the sea hides more than it reveals.
cards.student_note: The chapter keeps the image half-seen, so the reader has to sit with uncertainty instead of a clean explanation.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: The Whale as a Dish
cards.summary: Melville follows the whale from hunted body to food, tracing the odd history of eating it.
cards.student_note: The chapter uses table talk to stretch whaling beyond the sea and into culture, appetite, and custom.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Shark Massacre
cards.summary: Night cutting-in turns the dead whale into a crowded scene of labor, blades, and attacking sharks.
cards.student_note: The job is technical, but the chapter makes the work feel exposed and unstable as the carcass draws violence to the ship.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Funeral
cards.summary: The stripped whale is sent adrift in a scene that reads like a burial, even as sharks continue to tear at it.
cards.student_note: The funeral language asks readers to see both the body's use and its death; the chapter does not let either fact disappear.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Sphynx
cards.summary: Ahab addresses the whale's severed head as if it could answer his questions.
cards.student_note: The stillness of the head gives the chapter its force: the captain wants meaning, but the object remains mute.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Monkey-Rope
cards.summary: Ishmael shows how the cutting-in work binds sailors together by rope and risk.
cards.student_note: The monkey-rope makes dependence literal: one man's footing, balance, and safety are tied to another's work.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: The Battering-Ram
cards.summary: Melville examines the sperm whale's head as a huge instrument of force.
cards.student_note: The chapter stays with shape and structure long enough to make anatomy feel both exact and overwhelming.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: The Great Heidelburgh Tun
cards.summary: The whale's head is compared to a great cask, with valuable matter stored inside it.
cards.student_note: The comparison turns anatomy into a working problem: the whale is not just a body, but a container the crew must open carefully.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: Cistern and Buckets
cards.summary: The crew bails the whale's head, turning it into a literal cistern for the work of extraction.
cards.student_note: The technical detail matters because this chapter shows whaling as hand labor, not abstraction; every bucket brings the price of the whale into view.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: The Prairie
cards.summary: Melville stretches the whale's head into a landscape and tests the limits of physiognomy.
cards.student_note: The chapter asks whether human habits of reading faces can really explain a creature as large and strange as a whale.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Pequod Meets the Virgin
cards.summary: The Pequod meets the Jungfrau, and the encounter exposes how hope, pride, and shortage shape a whaling chase.
cards.student_note: The German crew's eagerness matters, but so does their lack of supplies; the scene makes ambition look fragile.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: The Honor and Glory of Whaling
cards.summary: Melville argues that whaling has ancient prestige and deserves to be treated as serious labor.
cards.student_note: The chapter is essay-like on purpose, but its larger aim is simple: whaling belongs in the company of honored human work.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: Jonah Historically Regarded
cards.summary: The chapter weighs skeptical responses to Jonah and uses them to probe what counts as belief.
cards.student_note: Instead of treating the story as a simple proof case, Melville turns it into a test of how readers judge religious history.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: Pitchpoling
cards.summary: Pitchpoling is presented as a swift, risky method for reaching a whale at distance.
cards.student_note: The explanation keeps the focus on motion and danger: the hunt depends on techniques that save time but raise the stakes.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: The Tail
cards.summary: Melville keeps circling the whale's tail, trying to describe its size, power, and motion.
cards.student_note: The chapter shows how hard it is to pin down a part that is always moving and hard to see clearly.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: Schools and Schoolmasters
cards.summary: Melville describes whale bands and uses the language of schooling to think about how whales gather and move.
cards.student_note: The chapter borrows human classroom terms, but it also shows how loosely they fit the whale world.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Pequod Meets the Rose-Bud
cards.summary: A foul-smelling whale drifts near the Rose-Bud, and Stubb jokes his way into the French captain's confidence before finding ambergris.
cards.student_note: The smell drives the scene, but the joke is not empty: the dead whale looks useless, then turns out to hide something valuable.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: A Squeeze of the Hand
cards.summary: As the crew squeezes cooled spermaceti, Ishmael turns the work into a calm, strangely tender meditation on touch and labor.
cards.student_note: The chapter begins with messy deck work and becomes a brief vision of shared labor that softens anger and distance.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Cassock
cards.summary: Melville gives the mincer a black whale-pelt cassock, making the blubber-room job look like a comic priestly office.
cards.student_note: The joke is visual, but it also makes the labor feel ritualized, as if work on the whale has its own ceremony.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: Stowing Down and Clearing Up
cards.summary: After the oil is stowed, the ship is cleaned and reset, and Ishmael reflects on toil, order, and the next return of danger.
cards.student_note: The spotless deck is only temporary; the chapter ends by reminding readers that another cry of 'There she blows' will undo it.

cards.title: Theatrical form: The Doubloon
cards.summary: Each crew member reads the same gold coin differently, turning the doubloon into prophecy, arithmetic, joke, or sermon.
cards.student_note: The scene shows that meaning depends on the reader: Ahab, Starbuck, Stubb, Flask, Queequeg, Fedallah, and Pip all see something else.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Leg and Arm
cards.summary: Ahab meets Captain Boomer of the Samuel Enderby, another whaler marked by Moby Dick, and the two men compare their missing limbs.
cards.student_note: The meeting makes Ahab's wound public: the whale has already taken an arm from one captain and a leg from the other.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Decanter
cards.summary: Ishmael turns from the Enderby itself to the food, drink, and provisions that shaped English and Dutch whaling life.
cards.student_note: The chapter is a historical digression, but it shows how the trade ran on heavy stores and a culture of abundance.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: A Bower in the Arsacides
cards.summary: Ishmael imagines a whale skeleton set up as a palm-shaded shrine in the Arsacides, then measures it like a relic.
cards.student_note: The skeleton is both a real specimen and a temple image, so the chapter keeps moving between reverence, measurement, and wonder.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: Measurement of the Whale’s Skeleton
cards.summary: Ishmael gives the Tranque skeleton's measurements and insists that bones alone cannot show the whale's living bulk.
cards.student_note: The numbers matter, but the chapter keeps warning that a skeleton can only hint at the whale's full size in motion.

cards.title: Whale bodies and whale knowledge: The Fossil Whale
cards.summary: From fossils and ancient carvings to Basilosaurus, Ishmael places whales in deep geological and mythic time.
cards.student_note: The chapter pushes the whale far beyond the voyage, linking modern whaling to fossils, temple art, and prehuman history.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Ahab’s Leg
cards.summary: After the Enderby meeting, Ahab's ivory leg causes real pain, and he orders a replacement before it fails him.
cards.student_note: The leg is not just a symbol here; it is a practical liability that keeps reminding readers how close obsession is to bodily damage.

cards.title: Narrative pressure: Ahab and Starbuck in the Cabin
cards.summary: Starbuck presses Ahab to repair leaking casks, but Ahab refuses and turns the leak into a version of his own condition.
cards.student_note: The cabin argument pits prudence against obsession, and Starbuck's warning only makes Ahab answer more fiercely.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Pequod Meets the Bachelor
cards.summary: The homeward-bound Bachelor arrives full of oil, music, and celebration, making the Pequod's grim purpose look even more isolated.
cards.student_note: The contrast is the point: one ship treats whaling as success and return, while Ahab sees only the chase ahead.

cards.title: Theatrical form: Midnight.⁠—The Forecastle Bulwarks
cards.summary: On a stormy night, Stubb and Flask lash down the anchors and talk themselves through fears about Ahab, lightning, and the ship's risk.
cards.student_note: The scene is comic on the surface, but it keeps circling back to the ship's exposure to weather and to Ahab's danger.

cards.title: Shipboard work and risk: The Deck
cards.summary: Ahab finds Queequeg's coffin being turned into a life-buoy and is shaken into thinking about how death and rescue can share one object.
cards.student_note: The chapter braids together coffin, life-buoy, and hatchway, so the deck itself feels like a place where meanings flip.

cards.title: Extracts: What to Notice
cards.summary: The extracts assemble centuries of whale references so the novel begins inside a noisy archive of human whale-thinking. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: This long opening collage quotes religious, classical, historical, scientific, and literary writing about whales. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The extracts teach students how to read the book's scale.

cards.title: Loomings: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael explains why he goes to sea when life on land feels stale, angry, or unbearable. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The novel begins with Ishmael's famous invitation to know him by name, then turns quickly to his restless need for the sea. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter establishes the narrator's voice: comic, learned, unstable, and surprisingly direct about depression and escape.

cards.title: The Carpetbag: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael travels toward Nantucket but stops in New Bedford, where the cold and darkness make the journey feel uncertain. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Ishmael sets out for Nantucket, the famous whaling port, but arrives first in New Bedford. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter slows the start just enough to make Ishmael vulnerable.

cards.title: The Spouter-Inn: What to Notice
cards.summary: At the Spouter-Inn, Ishmael is forced to share a bed with Queequeg and begins to rethink his fear of strangers. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Ishmael enters a strange inn full of whaling signs, stories, and unsettling art. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is the first major test of Ishmael's assumptions.

cards.title: The Counterpane: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael wakes beside Queequeg and connects the moment to childhood memories of fear, touch, and comfort. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The morning after the awkward bed-sharing arrangement, Ishmael finds Queequeg's arm around him. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter deepens Ishmael's emotional life and turns Queequeg from a frightening stranger into a source of unexpected tenderness.

cards.title: Breakfast: What to Notice
cards.summary: The inn's whalemen eat together in awkward silence, puncturing Ishmael's expectations of rough sailor talk. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Ishmael expects the whalers to be loud, wild storytellers, but breakfast is stiff and quiet. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter humanizes whalemen before romanticizing them.

cards.title: The Street: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael describes New Bedford as a place shaped by whaling money, global trade, and social contradiction. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Walking through New Bedford, Ishmael sees wealth, fashion, sailors, and global influence packed into a whaling town. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. Students get early context for the industry behind the adventure.

cards.title: The Chapel: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael enters a whalemen's chapel where memorial tablets make the danger of the voyage impossible to ignore. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The chapel is filled with memorials to sailors lost at sea. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter sets a funeral shadow over the adventure.

cards.title: The Pulpit: What to Notice
cards.summary: Father Mapple climbs into a ship-shaped pulpit, turning worship into a maritime drama. The chapter's pressure gathers around warning, judgment, belief, and inherited sacred language.
cards.student_note: Before the sermon begins, Melville makes the pulpit itself symbolic: it resembles a ship, and Father Mapple climbs into it like a sailor boarding. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter shows how form matters in this novel.

cards.title: The Sermon: What to Notice
cards.summary: Father Mapple retells Jonah as a lesson about fleeing duty, facing judgment, and speaking truth. The chapter's pressure gathers around warning, judgment, belief, and inherited sacred language.
cards.student_note: Father Mapple preaches on Jonah, who tries to escape God's command and is swallowed by a great fish. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The sermon gives students a key biblical pattern for the whole novel.

cards.title: Nightgown: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael and Queequeg share warmth and talk in bed, deepening their odd but sincere friendship. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The chapter pauses the plot for a quiet domestic scene. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter lets students see intimacy before the voyage hardens into labor and danger.

cards.title: Biographical: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael sketches Queequeg's royal background and his decision to leave home for whaling. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Queequeg is presented as the son of a king who chose to travel in order to learn about the wider world. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter gives Queequeg a history and motive.

cards.title: Wheelbarrow: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael and Queequeg travel toward Nantucket, and Queequeg proves calm and brave during a ferry accident. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The pair leave New Bedford and head for Nantucket. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. Queequeg's courage becomes practical rather than merely described.

cards.title: Nantucket: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael introduces Nantucket as a tiny island with an outsized whaling empire. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: Nantucket is described as bare, sandy, and improbably powerful. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. Students get the historical and imaginative launch point for the Pequod.

cards.title: Chowder: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael and Queequeg arrive at the Try Pots inn and enter Nantucket's whaling culture through food and talk. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The Try Pots gives the travelers warmth, chowder, and another threshold into the whaling world. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter offers relief before the signing of the ship.

cards.title: The Ramadan: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael anxiously waits through Queequeg's religious fast and misunderstands what is happening. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Queequeg observes a ritual fast, and Ishmael responds with confusion, worry, and comic impatience. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter is useful for discussing cross-cultural friendship without pretending Ishmael has become fully enlightened.

cards.title: The Prophet: What to Notice
cards.summary: A strange man named Elijah warns Ishmael and Queequeg that Ahab's voyage carries hidden danger. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Elijah speaks in riddles about Ahab and the Pequod, suggesting that the voyage is already shadowed by doom. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter adds prophecy and uncertainty before the ship leaves port.

cards.title: All Astir: What to Notice
cards.summary: The Pequod is readied for departure while Ahab remains unseen. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Supplies, crew, and shipboard routines gather into motion. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter turns preparation into suspense.

cards.title: Going Aboard: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael and Queequeg board the Pequod before dawn, with Elijah's warnings still hanging over them. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The chapter brings the two friends onto the ship in darkness and uncertainty. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The shore world is ending.

cards.title: Merry Christmas: What to Notice
cards.summary: The Pequod leaves Nantucket on Christmas Day under hard weather and harder command. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The ship departs in winter, with Peleg and Bildad shouting, praying, and managing the launch. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The voyage finally begins.

cards.title: The Lee Shore: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael briefly honors Bulkington, whose courage lies in choosing the open sea over false safety. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: This short symbolic chapter interrupts the voyage to meditate on Bulkington. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter gives students one of Melville's clearest statements about danger and integrity.

cards.title: Postscript: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael jokes that coronation oil comes from sperm whales, so whalers secretly supply royal ceremony. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: He extends his defense of whaling by suggesting that kings and queens are anointed with sperm oil at coronations. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This short add-on keeps the satire going and links whaling to monarchy, status, and public ceremony.

cards.title: Ahab: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab finally appears, marked by injury, command, and a disturbing stillness. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: After many delays, Ishmael sees Ahab on deck. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is the long-awaited entrance of the captain.

cards.title: Enter Ahab; To Him, Stubb: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab clashes with Stubb, showing that even ordinary shipboard correction can become threatening under his command. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Stubb lightly objects to Ahab's pacing, but Ahab's response turns the exchange into a warning. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter gives an early sign of the command climate aboard the Pequod.

cards.title: The Pipe: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab gives up smoking because even ordinary comfort no longer fits his obsession. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Ahab tries to smoke his pipe, then throws it away. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This brief chapter is a precise character study.

cards.title: Queen Mab: What to Notice
cards.summary: Stubb describes a strange dream that turns fear of Ahab into comic prophecy. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Stubb processes Ahab's aggression through a dream and a joking conversation with Flask. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter adds another warning system before Ahab states his mission.

cards.title: The Specksnyder: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael explains why harpooneers matter so much on whaling ships and how shipboard rank works. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: He describes the old Dutch office of specksnyder, or chief harpooneer, to show that whaling has its own rank structure. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. Students get a clear look at shipboard hierarchy before the cabin scenes begin.

cards.title: The Cabin-Table: What to Notice
cards.summary: Dinner aboard the Pequod reveals strict social order at the captain's table and a rougher one for the harpooneers. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: Ahab, Starbuck, Stubb, and Flask eat in formal silence, with the captain's authority shaping every movement at the table. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter makes the ship's hierarchy visible through meal customs.

cards.title: The Masthead: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael describes the long, dreamy, and dangerous work of keeping lookout from the masthead. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: He explains how whale ships keep men aloft for hours at a time, often for months across a voyage. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter slows the action into a study of perception and risk.

cards.title: Sunset: What to Notice
cards.summary: Alone at the stern windows, Ahab talks to himself about his pain, his power, and his refusal to stop. The chapter's pressure gathers around voice, staging, and the chapter's dramatic timing.
cards.student_note: In a soliloquy, Ahab sees his own life as a burden of iron, not gold. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter lets students hear Ahab's inner voice without the crew around him.

cards.title: Dusk: What to Notice
cards.summary: Starbuck worries that Ahab is dragging him into evil but still feels bound to obey. The chapter's pressure gathers around voice, staging, and the chapter's dramatic timing.
cards.student_note: Starbuck thinks through his moral conflict: he sees Ahab's madness, hates it, and yet cannot free himself from command or pity. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is one of the clearest Ahab-Starbuck counterpoints in the novel.

cards.title: First Night-Watch: What to Notice
cards.summary: Stubb works aloft and laughs off the unease below, insisting that whatever happens is already fated. The chapter's pressure gathers around voice, staging, and the chapter's dramatic timing.
cards.student_note: Alone at work, Stubb turns anxiety into jokes and song. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter gives Stubb his defining tone: comic fatalism.

cards.title: Moby Dick: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael gathers stories about the White Whale and explains why Ahab's hatred has become absolute. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives Moby Dick a reputation before the whale appears in the plot. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is the explanatory bridge between the oath and the hunt.

cards.title: Hark!: What to Notice
cards.summary: On a quiet night watch, the crew hears strange noises below deck and wonders who or what is hidden aboard. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Archy thinks he hears coughing and movement under the hatches, and Cabaco brushes it off as nerves and overeating. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter is all tension and no payoff, which is the point.

cards.title: The Chart: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab studies charts and ocean patterns, trying to make the whale hunt seem calculable. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Ahab's private planning becomes visible as he tracks seasons, cruising grounds, and whale movements. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter connects madness to method.

cards.title: The Affidavit: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael builds a case for the real power and danger of sperm whales by piling up witness-like evidence. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Written like a sworn statement, the chapter gathers sightings, named whales, shipwrecks, and other examples. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is one of the novel's clearest mixes of fact and argument.

cards.title: Surmises: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab thinks through how to keep the crew's ordinary whaling work aligned with his private revenge. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Ahab knows the crew still expects profit, food, routine, and whale oil. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter clarifies the political problem of Ahab's command.

cards.title: The Mat-Maker: What to Notice
cards.summary: While weaving with Queequeg, Ishmael turns the work into a meditation on fate before whales are sighted. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: A quiet shipboard task becomes philosophical as Ishmael imagines the mat as a loom of fate, with free will, chance, and necessity crossing one another. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter is classic Melville: labor becomes thought, and thought is tested by events.

cards.title: The First Lowering: What to Notice
cards.summary: The first whale chase reveals the danger of the work and the hidden presence of Ahab's private boat crew. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The Pequod lowers boats for the first time, and the chaos of whaling becomes immediate. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter turns whaling from concept into action.

cards.title: The Hyena: What to Notice
cards.summary: After a frightening accident, Ishmael slips into grim humor and treats the voyage like a death sentence he can laugh at. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Ishmael says there are moments when life feels like a cruel joke, and whaling is one of them. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter shows how the novel turns fear into dark comedy instead of denying it.

cards.title: Ahab’s Boat and Crew. Fedallah: What to Notice
cards.summary: The chapter explains Ahab's hidden boat crew and Fedallah's unsettling closeness to him.
cards.student_note: Fedallah becomes part of the novel's doom structure, but the immediate problem is practical: Ahab has been keeping a private crew inside a public voyage.

cards.title: The Spirit-Spout: What to Notice
cards.summary: A mysterious spout appears at night, seeming to lure the Pequod onward. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The crew repeatedly sees a ghostly whale spout, but the whale itself remains out of reach. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter builds atmosphere and momentum.

cards.title: The Gam: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael explains the custom of a gam, the social visit whaling ships make when they meet at sea. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: Whaleships exchange hails, letters, news, and boat visits when they cross paths. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter gives students a key piece of whaling culture and a name for one of the novel's most important social rituals.

cards.title: The Town-Ho ’s Story: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael tells the long story of the Town-Ho, where abuse, mutiny, secrecy, and Moby Dick converge. The chapter's pressure gathers around voice, staging, and the chapter's dramatic timing.
cards.student_note: Framed as a tale told in Lima, the chapter follows the Town-Ho from its leaking hull to Radney's abuse of Steelkilt, the resulting revolt, and the later whale chase in which Moby Dick kills Radney. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is one of the novel's biggest stories within the story.

cards.title: Of the Monstrous Pictures of Whales: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael attacks wrong pictures of whales, from old religious art to modern books and scientific drawings. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He moves through examples from sculpture, classical painting, Bible illustrations, and natural history books, arguing that artists usually get whales wrong because they work from guesswork or damaged bodies. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter makes representation itself a classroom issue.

cards.title: Of the Less Erroneous Pictures of Whales, and the True Pictures of Whaling Scenes: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael names better whale pictures and argues that the best ones show action instead of stiff outlines. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He compares artists and naturalists, praising the pictures that actually catch the motion of whaling scenes. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter continues the book's argument about how to see and represent the whale.

cards.title: Of Whales in Paint; in Teeth; in Wood; in Sheet-Iron; in Stone; in Mountains; in Stars: What to Notice
cards.summary: Melville shows whales turning up everywhere in art, landscape, and even the night sky. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: The chapter starts with a painted sign of a whale attack, then moves through carvings on teeth and wood, metal whales on church spires, rock shapes, mountain ridges, and constellations. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is one of the book's clearest examples of how obsession changes perception.

cards.title: Brit: What to Notice
cards.summary: The Pequod sails through fields of brit, the tiny food whales eat, and the scene opens onto the sea's violence. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: Right whales feed in the brit like mowers moving through a field, but the calm image gives way to a harsher thought: the sea destroys ships, whales, and people without mercy. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter links whale life to the book's larger philosophy of danger and vulnerability.

cards.title: Squid: What to Notice
cards.summary: In the still sea, the crew spots a giant white creature that turns out to be a live squid, not Moby Dick. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Daggoo sees a strange white mass rising and sinking ahead of the ship, and the men rush to the yards thinking they may have found the White Whale. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter expands the ocean beyond the whale hunt itself.

cards.title: The Line: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael explains the whale-line, the dangerous rope that can drag a man or boat to death during a chase. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: He describes the line's material, length, coiling, and the way it is laid through the boat before a hunt. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter turns a technical detail into a lesson about whaling's violence.

cards.title: Stubb Kills a Whale: What to Notice
cards.summary: On a still day at sea, Stubb spots a sperm whale, snaps the crew into action, and helps bring it down. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The chapter starts in a dreamy calm, then suddenly shifts when the whale appears under the ship. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter shows Stubb's mix of humor and competence under pressure.

cards.title: The Dart: What to Notice
cards.summary: Melville explains how hard it is for a harpooneer to row, shout, turn, and throw the harpoon all at once. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The chapter describes the normal boat sequence in a whale chase: the harpooneer rows under pressure, then has to twist around and hurl the dart at the whale at exactly the right instant. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This technical chapter makes the chase scenes easier to read and much more physical.

cards.title: The Crotch: What to Notice
cards.summary: Melville explains the harpoon rest at the bow and how the second iron can become dangerous during a chase. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The crotch is the notched stick that holds the harpoon ready at the front of the boat, usually with two irons waiting. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter gives students the gear knowledge they need for later boat scenes.

cards.title: The Whale as a Dish: What to Notice
cards.summary: Melville asks why eating whale seems strange when people accept other kinds of animal flesh. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: He gives examples of whale dishes, from right-whale tongue in France to sailors dipping biscuit in whale oil, and even describes brains as food. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter connects appetite to violence and exposes double standards around eating.

cards.title: The Shark Massacre: What to Notice
cards.summary: While the crew waits to cut in the whale, sharks swarm the carcass and the men beat them back with spades. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: Stubb sets an anchor watch so the whale can be left overnight, but the sharks gather in such numbers that Queequeg and another sailor have to attack them under lantern light. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter keeps whale processing from feeling neat or heroic.

cards.title: The Blanket: What to Notice
