cards.summary: Ishmael argues that a whale's blubber works like skin and a blanket, making the outer layer hard to define. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He works through the strange question of where the whale's skin really is and compares the blubber to a flexible covering that can be stripped away in pieces. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. Melville uses the whale's body to show how knowledge in this book is often indirect and unstable.

cards.title: The Funeral: What to Notice
cards.summary: The beheaded whale drifts away like a grotesque funeral procession, stripped by sharks and seabirds. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The whale's body floats off white and huge, while sharks tear at it below and birds attack it from above. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter turns a dead whale into a symbol of public mourning, theft, and superstition.

cards.title: The Sphynx: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab talks to the hoisted sperm whale head like a silent sphinx and tries to force meaning from it. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The detached head becomes a mute monument, and Ahab launches into one of his fiercest speeches at it. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter shows Ahab's habit of treating the world as if it must answer him.

cards.title: The Monkey-Rope: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael is tied to Queequeg by a monkey-rope while Queequeg works on the whale's back. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: Ishmael has to hold Queequeg by a rope while Queequeg stands on the whale during cutting-in. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is one of the novel's clearest metaphors for human interdependence.

cards.title: Stubb and Flask Kill a Right Whale; and Then Have a Talk Over Him: What to Notice
cards.summary: Stubb and Flask kill a right whale, then joke about Fedallah and the strange luck hanging over the ship. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The Pequod drifts into a patch of right whales, and the two mates bring one alongside after a hard chase around the ship. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter shows ordinary shipwork continuing even when the novel pauses for whale anatomy.

cards.title: The Battering-Ram: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael studies the whale's head as a massive battering ram built to take violent impact. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: Melville asks the reader to look closely at the sperm whale's front end, which is a broad, boneless wall of toughness. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is one of Melville's clearest mock-scientific arguments about the whale's anatomy.

cards.title: The Great Heidelburgh Tun: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael compares the whale's head to a giant wine cask packed with spermaceti. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He breaks the sperm whale's head into parts, especially the junk and the case, and explains how the upper chamber holds the valuable spermaceti. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter makes the whale's body into both a scientific object and a container of wealth.

cards.title: Cistern and Buckets: What to Notice
cards.summary: Tashtego lowers buckets into the sperm whale's head for oil, but a mishap sends him tumbling inside. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The whale's head becomes a kind of cistern, and the crew works bucket by bucket to drain it. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter turns anatomy into action and danger at once.

cards.title: The Prairie: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael tries to read the whale's face, but the sperm whale resists human systems for interpreting faces. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: Using the language of physiognomy and phrenology, Ishmael argues that the sperm whale has no normal face to study: no nose, no readable features, only a broad forehead that looks both sublime and unknowable. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter shows how badly human categories fit the whale.

cards.title: The Nut: What to Notice
cards.summary: The whale's tiny hidden brain and huge spinal cord make its body seem powerful in a way human categories miss. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: Ishmael says the sperm whale's brain is small and deeply hidden, then shifts attention to the spine and hump as possible sources of strength and character. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter keeps pushing against simple ideas of intelligence and power.

cards.title: The Pequod Meets the Virgin: What to Notice
cards.summary: The Pequod meets the German ship Jungfrau, and both crews race after a wounded old whale. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: Derick De Deer comes aboard begging for oil because his ship is empty, then immediately throws himself into the chase when whales are sighted. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter widens the voyage beyond the Pequod and shows how whaling connects ships from different nations in the same violent economy.

cards.title: The Honor and Glory of Whaling: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael defends whaling as an ancient, honorable calling linked to heroes, saints, and gods. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He traces whaling back through Perseus, Saint George, Hercules, Jonah, and Vishnu, arguing that the trade belongs to a long and distinguished tradition. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter is Melville's direct defense of his subject.

cards.title: Jonah Historically Regarded: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael treats the story of Jonah and the whale as something people debate like history, not just faith. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He collects skeptical and defensive explanations for Jonah's journey, including arguments about whale anatomy, the distance to Nineveh, and whether Jonah could have reached the city by another route. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter shows Melville mixing scholarship, satire, and biblical commentary.

cards.title: Pitchpoling: What to Notice
cards.summary: Stubb uses the special whaling technique of pitchpoling to strike a fast-moving whale from a rocking boat. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: After Queequeg oils the boat to help it slide, the crew chases a running whale. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter turns whaling into exact, high-risk labor instead of vague adventure.

cards.title: The Fountain: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael tries to explain the whale's spout and ends up circling around a mystery he cannot fully settle. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He argues about how the sperm whale breathes, whether the spout is water or vapor, and why the animal must rise to the surface at regular intervals. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is one of the book's clearest chapters about the limits of knowledge.

cards.title: The Tail: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael gives the whale's tail a full anatomical and symbolic study. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He describes the tail's size, structure, strength, and the different ways a whale uses it to move, strike, sweep, play, and dive. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. Melville turns a single body part into a lesson in form, force, and interpretation.

cards.title: The Grand Armada: What to Notice
cards.summary: The Pequod sails into a huge herd of sperm whales, and a dangerous chase becomes a crowded spectacle. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: Near the Straits of Sunda, the ship enters waters filled with whales, then has to push through a dense moving mass of spouts, bodies, and panic. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter enlarges the scale of the voyage and shows the world of whaling as global, crowded, and unstable.

cards.title: Schools and Schoolmasters: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael explains sperm whale schools: female groups, young male bands, and older bulls who live mostly alone. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: Melville turns whale behavior into a social lesson. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter deepens the book's cetology and gives students a clear example of Melville using animal life to think about gender, age, and social order.

cards.title: The Pequod Meets the Rose-Bud: What to Notice
cards.summary: The Pequod meets the Rose-Bud, a French whaler stuck with foul carcasses, and Stubb finds ambergris. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: Stubb approaches a French ship carrying two bad catches: one blasted whale and one dried-out carcass that still might contain ambergris. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter mixes comedy, commerce, and decay.

cards.title: Ambergris: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael explains ambergris, the waxy perfume material found in whales, and argues that whalers are unfairly blamed for bad smells. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: He defines ambergris, notes that it is prized in perfume and sometimes cooking, and traces the bad smell associated with whaling to older methods of handling whale oil. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This digression matters because Melville keeps turning the whale into a scientific and commercial object.

cards.title: A Squeeze of the Hand: What to Notice
cards.summary: While squeezing spermaceti by hand, Ishmael is briefly overwhelmed by warmth and fellowship. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The work of pressing the whale oil back into fluid becomes oddly soothing, even affectionate. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter matters because it makes physical labor shape emotion and thought.

cards.title: The Cassock: What to Notice
cards.summary: The blubber stripped from the whale becomes a black robe, and the mincer wears it like a cassock while cutting it up. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: Melville describes the long strip of whale skin pulled over the mincer's body, then shows him standing at the wooden horse where blubber is chopped into pieces for the pots. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter is a sharp example of Melville turning labor into ritual imagery.

cards.title: The Try-Works: What to Notice
cards.summary: The ship's furnaces turn whale blubber into oil, and Ishmael turns the scene into a nightmare of labor and fire. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The try-works make the Pequod into a floating factory at night. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter is essential for understanding whaling as industrial labor.

cards.title: Stowing Down and Clearing Up: What to Notice
cards.summary: After the oil is casked and the ship is cleaned, the Pequod is spotless only until the next whale. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: Melville walks through the process of stowing the oil below deck and scrubbing away the blood, soot, and blubber above. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter captures the grind of whale work better than any single chase scene.

cards.title: The Doubloon: What to Notice
cards.summary: Different crew members interpret Ahab's gold coin according to their own fears, desires, and beliefs. The chapter's pressure gathers around voice, staging, and the chapter's dramatic timing.
cards.student_note: The coin nailed to the mast becomes a kind of mirror. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter is a compact lesson in interpretation.

cards.title: Leg and Arm: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab meets the captain of the Samuel Enderby, another whaleman injured by Moby Dick. The chapter's pressure gathers around voice, staging, and the chapter's dramatic timing.
cards.student_note: The two wounded captains compare injuries, and the English captain tells the story of losing his arm in a whale attack. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter gives Ahab another version of obsession's cost in the body.

cards.title: The Decanter: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael praises the Enderby whaling house and recalls a cheerful visit to the Samuel Enderby. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: He explains how the Enderbys helped open new whale grounds, then recalls a convivial night aboard the Samuel Enderby with flip, beef, bread, and plenty of noise. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter places whaling inside a larger history of trade, national rivalry, and shipboard abundance.

cards.title: A Bower in the Arsacides: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael describes a giant sperm whale skeleton in a palm grove and shows how little a dead frame reveals. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He says he learned the whale's bones from a stranded skeleton in the Arsacides, where the priests treat it like a sacred object. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter pushes the book deeper into whale anatomy and display.

cards.title: Measurement of the Whale’s Skeleton: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael gives exact measurements for a whale skeleton while showing that bones still understate the living animal. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He compares the Tranque whale's length, ribs, spine, and skull to the larger body it once held, then points out how much flesh, weight, and force are missing from the bones. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter teaches students how Melville can sound scientific while still insisting on uncertainty.

cards.title: The Fossil Whale: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael moves from living whales to fossil whales and stretches the book into deep geological time. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He gathers examples of ancient whale bones, compares them with modern whale anatomy, and describes a huge extinct whale found in Alabama that was first misread as a reptile. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter makes the whale feel ancient enough to outlast ordinary human history.

cards.title: Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish?⁠—Will He Perish?: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ishmael asks whether whales have shrunk over time or may eventually be hunted out of existence. The chapter's pressure gathers around whale bodies, evidence, and the limits of classification.
cards.student_note: He weighs old claims about gigantic whales against newer measurements, then takes up the harder question of whether relentless hunting could wipe the whale out. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter turns whale biology into a debate about extinction and human damage.

cards.title: Ahab’s Leg: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab's damaged ivory leg reminds the crew how physically dependent and dangerously driven he is. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Ahab's artificial leg has been strained by the voyage, and its repair draws attention to his injury. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter keeps Ahab embodied rather than abstract.

cards.title: The Carpenter: What to Notice
cards.summary: Melville introduces the Pequod's carpenter as a practical fixer who can make almost anything needed on a whaleship. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: The carpenter works at his vice-bench repairing, shaping, drilling, and improvising across many kinds of shipboard tasks. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter shows labor as intelligence, not just background work.

cards.title: Ahab and Starbuck in the Cabin: What to Notice
cards.summary: Starbuck confronts Ahab over leaking oil, briefly challenging revenge in the name of duty. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: When the ship's oil casks leak, Starbuck argues that the crew must stop and repair them. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is a key Ahab-Starbuck conflict.

cards.title: The Pacific: What to Notice
cards.summary: The Pequod enters the Pacific, whose calm beauty briefly makes Ahab's obsession visible against something larger. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Ishmael describes the Pacific as a vast, mysterious sea that seems alive with memory and dreaming. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter gives the voyage a change of atmosphere and a wider world.

cards.title: The Blacksmith: What to Notice
cards.summary: Perth, the ship's blacksmith, is revealed as a ruined man whose pain and labor have driven him to whaling. The chapter's pressure gathers around labor, tools, bodies, and command pressure.
cards.student_note: While he keeps repairing weapons and tools on deck, Ishmael tells the story of how drink, loss, and poverty broke his family and his body. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter gives one of the book's clearest portraits of damaged labor.

cards.title: The Gilder: What to Notice
cards.summary: In a rare golden calm, the sea seems beautiful enough to soften Ahab, Starbuck, and Stubb for a moment. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The Pequod works through long stretches of quiet weather, and the ocean looks almost gentle and pastoral. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter balances the novel's violence with a moment of beauty.

cards.title: The Pequod Meets the Bachelor: What to Notice
cards.summary: The grim Pequod meets the cheerful, full-of-oil Bachelor, and Ahab rejects its easy confidence. The chapter's pressure gathers around voice, staging, and the chapter's dramatic timing.
cards.student_note: The Bachelor is homeward-bound, richly loaded, and almost festive, with a captain who does not believe in the White Whale. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This encounter sharpens the contrast between plenty and obsession, celebration and doom.

cards.title: The Dying Whale: What to Notice
cards.summary: Watching a whale die, Ahab turns the scene into a meditation on sun worship, death, and nature. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: After a successful hunt, Ahab sees the whale turn its head toward the sun as it dies, then reflects on how life seems to seek light even at the end. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter gives Ahab one of his more reflective moments, but it does not soften him.

cards.title: The Whale Watch: What to Notice
cards.summary: Fedallah gives Ahab prophecies that seem to promise safety while actually tightening doom. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Ahab and Fedallah speak at night about signs, hearses, hemp, and death. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter is crucial for the ending because its riddles return during the chase.

cards.title: Midnight.⁠—The Forecastle Bulwarks: What to Notice
cards.summary: Stubb and Flask joke through the storm while lashing down anchors, turning danger into rough shipboard comedy. The chapter's pressure gathers around voice, staging, and the chapter's dramatic timing.
cards.student_note: The two mates argue about risk, insurance, lightning, and whether Ahab's ship is really any more dangerous than others. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This chapter shows how sailors manage fear with wit and technical talk.

cards.title: The Musket: What to Notice
cards.summary: Starbuck considers killing Ahab but cannot bring himself to do it. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: With Ahab asleep and a loaded musket nearby, Starbuck sees a possible way to save the ship. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is Starbuck's sharpest crisis.

cards.title: The Deck: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab and the carpenter speak over the coffin-life-buoy, mixing practical work with grim symbolism. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The carpenter treats the coffin-life-buoy as a job; Ahab cannot help reading it as a sign. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter keeps symbolic pressure high without leaving shipboard labor behind.

cards.title: The Cabin: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab pushes Pip away, fearing the boy's broken insight will weaken his resolve. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Pip's attachment to Ahab creates a moment of tenderness and danger. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The chapter shows Ahab resisting compassion because it threatens the hunt.

cards.title: The Hat: What to Notice
cards.summary: Ahab stands isolated as signs gather and the ship nears the White Whale. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: The chapter focuses on Ahab's exposed figure and the ominous theft of his hat by a sea bird. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. The final atmosphere tightens here.

cards.title: The Pequod Meets the Delight: What to Notice
cards.summary: The Delight brings news of disaster from Moby Dick, but Ahab treats the warning as confirmation. The chapter's pressure gathers around the local details that steer student judgment.
cards.student_note: Another ship has encountered the White Whale and suffered for it. Its local details change how the chapter should be read in sequence. This is the last major warning before the chase.

cards.title: The Lamp and Whale Oil
cards.summary: The Lamp shows whale oil lighting the sailors' sleeping quarters after the try-works chapters.
cards.student_note: The tiny chapter makes extraction intimate: the product of the whale hunt becomes ordinary light inside the ship.

cards.title: The Lamp: working action
cards.summary: The whaleship's lamps burn whale oil, so even the sailors' sleeping quarters glow with the product of the hunt. The compact form lets working action carry more pressure than the chapter's length suggests.
cards.student_note: Ishmael describes the forecastle lit by lamps filled at the try-works, as if the ship were a small shrine. working action gives the short chapter a concrete role in the surrounding sequence. The chapter makes the connection between whales and everyday illumination impossible to miss.

cards.title: The Deck Towards the End of the First Night Watch: brief stage cue
cards.summary: A short storm exchange shows Starbuck urging practical caution while Ahab turns command into defiance.
cards.student_note: The scene matters because seamanship and theatrical will collide in just a few lines.

cards.title: The Deck Towards the End of the First Night Watch: compressed voice
cards.summary: Starbuck tries to manage the storm-ready ship, but Ahab dismisses practical caution. The compact form lets compressed voice carry more pressure than the chapter's length suggests.
cards.student_note: In a short stage-like exchange, Starbuck asks to secure the yards and anchors, while Ahab refuses to strike anything down. compressed voice gives the short chapter a concrete role in the surrounding sequence. The chapter shows command turning into performance.

cards.title: Midnight Aloft.⁠—Thunder and Lightning: brief scene turn
cards.summary: High in the storm, Tashtego keeps watch while the ship moves deeper into danger. The compact form lets brief scene turn carry more pressure than the chapter's length suggests.
cards.student_note: This short chapter holds the storm from the masthead. brief scene turn gives the short chapter a concrete role in the surrounding sequence. The chapter keeps the storm sequence moving through multiple viewpoints.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Extracts
cards.summary: This long opening collage quotes religious, classical, historical, scientific, and literary writing about whales. It is not a normal preface; it is a crowded library that makes the whale feel ancient, global, symbolic, commercial, and impossible to pin down.
cards.student_note: The extracts teach students how to read the book's scale. Melville wants the whale to exceed one genre or explanation, so he opens with many voices before giving us Ishmael's.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Loomings
cards.summary: The novel begins with Ishmael's famous invitation to know him by name, then turns quickly to his restless need for the sea. He presents whaling not as a simple adventure but as a remedy for despair, boredom, and violent impulses.
cards.student_note: This chapter establishes the narrator's voice: comic, learned, unstable, and surprisingly direct about depression and escape. It also makes the voyage psychological before it becomes nautical.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Carpetbag
cards.summary: Ishmael sets out for Nantucket, the famous whaling port, but arrives first in New Bedford. The chapter turns a practical travel problem into atmosphere: winter, poverty, and strangeness gather around him before he ever reaches a ship.
cards.student_note: The chapter slows the start just enough to make Ishmael vulnerable. Students meet the world of whaling through discomfort and improvisation rather than heroic certainty.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Spouter-Inn
cards.summary: Ishmael enters a strange inn full of whaling signs, stories, and unsettling art. His fear peaks when he learns he must share a bed with Queequeg, a harpooneer he has not met, but the encounter begins moving from panic toward trust.
cards.student_note: This is the first major test of Ishmael's assumptions. Melville uses comedy and fear to begin one of the book's central relationships.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Counterpane
cards.summary: The morning after the awkward bed-sharing arrangement, Ishmael finds Queequeg's arm around him. Instead of treating the scene only as a joke, he links it to a childhood memory of punishment and loneliness.
cards.student_note: The chapter deepens Ishmael's emotional life and turns Queequeg from a frightening stranger into a source of unexpected tenderness.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Breakfast
cards.summary: Ishmael expects the whalers to be loud, wild storytellers, but breakfast is stiff and quiet. The comedy comes from the gap between his imagination and the ordinary social awkwardness of the room.
cards.student_note: The chapter humanizes whalemen before romanticizing them. It also shows that Ishmael's narration often corrects itself.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Street
cards.summary: Walking through New Bedford, Ishmael sees wealth, fashion, sailors, and global influence packed into a whaling town. The chapter makes whaling feel like an economic system, not just a dangerous job.
cards.student_note: Students get early context for the industry behind the adventure. The chapter also hints that the voyage is tied to money, empire, and global movement.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Chapel
cards.summary: The chapel is filled with memorials to sailors lost at sea. Ishmael confronts the reality that whaling can erase bodies, families, and certainty.
cards.student_note: This chapter sets a funeral shadow over the adventure. It also prepares the way for Father Mapple's sermon and the book's recurring biblical frame.

cards.title: Authority and belief: The Pulpit
cards.summary: Before the sermon begins, Melville makes the pulpit itself symbolic: it resembles a ship, and Father Mapple climbs into it like a sailor boarding. Religious teaching and sea travel are visually fused.
cards.student_note: The chapter shows how form matters in this novel. Spaces, objects, and gestures often become arguments before anyone explains them.

cards.title: Authority and belief: The Sermon
cards.summary: Father Mapple preaches on Jonah, who tries to escape God's command and is swallowed by a great fish. The sermon turns obedience, guilt, and truth-telling into urgent questions for anyone going to sea.
cards.student_note: The sermon gives students a key biblical pattern for the whole novel. It also raises the question of whether Ahab, Ishmael, or anyone else can recognize a warning in time.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: A Bosom Friend
cards.summary: Ishmael decides that Queequeg's supposed savagery is less troubling than many civilized people's cruelty. Their friendship becomes affectionate, funny, and serious at once.
cards.student_note: This chapter is essential for understanding Ishmael's moral education. It also complicates the book's racial and cultural assumptions without making them disappear.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Nightgown
cards.summary: The chapter pauses the plot for a quiet domestic scene. Ishmael and Queequeg smoke, talk, and sit together under the bedclothes while the cold world remains outside.
cards.student_note: This chapter lets students see intimacy before the voyage hardens into labor and danger. It also keeps Queequeg from being treated only as a symbol or exotic figure.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Biographical
cards.summary: Queequeg is presented as the son of a king who chose to travel in order to learn about the wider world. His whaling life is not random; it is tied to ambition, exile, and curiosity.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives Queequeg a history and motive. It asks students to see him as a full character, not simply as Ishmael's first surprise.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Wheelbarrow
cards.summary: The pair leave New Bedford and head for Nantucket. A comic travel episode turns serious when Queequeg rescues a frightened passenger after a mishap on the water.
cards.student_note: Queequeg's courage becomes practical rather than merely described. The chapter also moves the story from shore friendship toward the whaling voyage.

cards.title: Work under pressure: Nantucket
cards.summary: Nantucket is described as bare, sandy, and improbably powerful. Ishmael turns the island into a comic marvel: a small place whose sailors range over the whole ocean.
cards.student_note: Students get the historical and imaginative launch point for the Pequod. The chapter makes geography feel like destiny.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Chowder
cards.summary: The Try Pots gives the travelers warmth, chowder, and another threshold into the whaling world. The ordinary meal becomes part of the book's dense attention to bodies, appetite, and sea labor.
cards.student_note: The chapter offers relief before the signing of the ship. It also keeps the world of the novel tactile and social.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Ship
cards.summary: The pair reaches Nantucket and chooses the Pequod, a ship decorated with the materials of whale hunting. Ishmael signs on without meeting Captain Ahab, whose absence makes him more mysterious.
cards.student_note: This chapter commits Ishmael and Queequeg to the voyage. It also introduces the Pequod as more than transportation: it is a symbolic world built from violence, labor, and profit.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Ramadan
cards.summary: The Ramadan shows Ishmael caring for Queequeg while misunderstanding Queequeg's religious practice.
cards.student_note: Care and confusion coexist here, which makes the cross-cultural friendship more complicated than simple tolerance.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: His Mark
cards.summary: Peleg and Bildad hesitate over Queequeg's religion, but his confidence and Ishmael's loyalty carry the moment. Queequeg signs with his mark and joins the voyage.
cards.student_note: The chapter tests whether the friendship can survive institutional suspicion. It also makes Queequeg's labor value impossible for the owners to ignore.

cards.title: Authority and belief: The Prophet
cards.summary: Elijah speaks in riddles about Ahab and the Pequod, suggesting that the voyage is already shadowed by doom. Ishmael cannot tell whether to take him seriously.
cards.student_note: The chapter adds prophecy and uncertainty before the ship leaves port. It trains readers to notice warnings that characters half-ignore.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: All Astir
cards.summary: Supplies, crew, and shipboard routines gather into motion. The practical work of departure contrasts with the mystery surrounding Ahab, who still does not appear.
cards.student_note: This chapter turns preparation into suspense. Students can see how Melville delays Ahab in order to make his eventual entrance heavier.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Going Aboard
cards.summary: The chapter brings the two friends onto the ship in darkness and uncertainty. Elijah appears again, making the departure feel less like a clean beginning than a crossing into danger.
cards.student_note: The shore world is ending. The chapter converts prophecy into atmosphere and makes boarding feel like commitment.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Merry Christmas
cards.summary: The ship departs in winter, with Peleg and Bildad shouting, praying, and managing the launch. Ahab is still absent, but the ship has entered the ocean's authority.
cards.student_note: The voyage finally begins. The Christmas setting adds irony: a day associated with birth opens a journey toward death.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Lee Shore
cards.summary: This short symbolic chapter interrupts the voyage to meditate on Bulkington. Ishmael imagines him as someone who refuses the safety of shore because truth may require exposure and risk.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives students one of Melville's clearest statements about danger and integrity. It also shows how even a minor character can become a philosophical emblem.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Advocate
cards.summary: He answers critics who call whaling dirty or unpoetic. He compares whale hunters to soldiers, points to the industry's wealth and reach, and says whalemen have helped open parts of the world that later travelers used too.
cards.student_note: The chapter is Melville's defense of his subject. It turns whaling into a way of thinking about labor, history, and empire.

cards.title: Work under pressure: Postscript
cards.summary: He extends his defense of whaling by suggesting that kings and queens are anointed with sperm oil at coronations. The tone is comic, but the point is serious: whaling supports rituals of power even while people look down on it.
cards.student_note: This short add-on keeps the satire going and links whaling to monarchy, status, and public ceremony.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Knights and Squires
cards.summary: Starbuck is presented as serious, conscientious, brave, and marked by thoughts of home. Ishmael then describes how each mate on the Pequod has a harpooneer as a kind of squire, turning ship hierarchy into a knightly order.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives students part of the crew map they need for the rest of the voyage. It also shows how Melville mixes social rank, labor, and symbolism.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Knights and Squires
cards.summary: Stubb is easygoing, humorous, and almost unnervingly calm in danger; Flask is short, combative, and eager to hunt whales. Ishmael then names Tashtego and Daggoo and notices that the Pequod is a mixed crew of outsiders, including Pip, whose later fate he quietly foreshadows.
cards.student_note: The chapter rounds out the officers and the ship's social structure. It also stresses that the Pequod is a global working crew, not a purely American one.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Ahab
cards.summary: After many delays, Ishmael sees Ahab on deck. His scar, ivory leg, and withdrawn intensity make him feel both human and almost mythic.
cards.student_note: This is the long-awaited entrance of the captain. The chapter teaches students to read Ahab's body and silence as part of his power.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Enter Ahab; To Him, Stubb
cards.summary: Stubb lightly objects to Ahab's pacing, but Ahab's response turns the exchange into a warning. The crew is learning that this captain's authority is personal, volatile, and difficult to challenge.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives an early sign of the command climate aboard the Pequod. It also introduces Stubb's humor as a coping style.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Pipe
cards.summary: Ahab tries to smoke his pipe, then throws it away. A habit associated with rest and reflection cannot survive the pressure of his purpose.
cards.student_note: This brief chapter is a precise character study. Ahab's obsession is not only dramatic; it has invaded the smallest rituals of daily life.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Queen Mab
cards.summary: Stubb processes Ahab's aggression through a dream and a joking conversation with Flask. The comedy does not erase the danger; it shows how sailors absorb fear and keep moving.
cards.student_note: The chapter adds another warning system before Ahab states his mission. It also lets students see humor as part of the novel's dark machinery.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Cetology
cards.summary: He reviews earlier whale writers, argues that the sperm whale deserves top billing, and then proposes a rough scheme that sorts whales by size into Folios, Octavos, and Duodecimos. The chapter is part joke, part real taxonomy, and part confession that complete knowledge is out of reach.
cards.student_note: This is one of the book's strangest formal chapters. It shows how Melville turns mock scholarship into a way of thinking about limits, order, and language.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Specksnyder
cards.summary: He describes the old Dutch office of specksnyder, or chief harpooneer, to show that whaling has its own rank structure. The chapter also sets up the rule that officers live aft and common sailors forward, while Ahab works inside those customs to strengthen his command.
cards.student_note: Students get a clear look at shipboard hierarchy before the cabin scenes begin. It also hints that Ahab knows how to use maritime custom while bending it to his own ends.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Cabin-Table
cards.summary: Ahab, Starbuck, Stubb, and Flask eat in formal silence, with the captain's authority shaping every movement at the table. After they leave, Queequeg, Tashtego, and Daggoo eat with much more noise and freedom, while the steward cowers between both worlds.
cards.student_note: The chapter makes the ship's hierarchy visible through meal customs. It also shows that Ahab's power is built into daily ritual, not just into orders.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Masthead
cards.summary: He explains how whale ships keep men aloft for hours at a time, often for months across a voyage. The height can feel calm and meditative, but it can also dull attention, and Ishmael warns that a lookout lost in thought can miss a whale or even lose his life.
cards.student_note: This chapter slows the action into a study of perception and risk. It also gives the novel one of its clearest meditations on distraction, imagination, and the danger of drifting from the task at hand.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Quarterdeck
cards.summary: Ahab gathers the crew and turns an ordinary whaling voyage into a hunt for one white whale. By nailing a gold coin to the mast and demanding the crew's oath, he converts labor into obsession.
cards.student_note: This is the central plot turn. The Pequod stops being merely a workplace and becomes the instrument of Ahab's private vengeance.

cards.title: Staged voice: Sunset
cards.summary: In a soliloquy, Ahab sees his own life as a burden of iron, not gold. He admits that beauty hurts him now, then turns back to his revenge and says he will keep driving toward it no matter what.
cards.student_note: This chapter lets students hear Ahab's inner voice without the crew around him. It sharpens the tragedy by showing both self-knowledge and total refusal.

cards.title: Staged voice: Dusk
cards.summary: Starbuck thinks through his moral conflict: he sees Ahab's madness, hates it, and yet cannot free himself from command or pity. The scene shifts from quiet reflection to the noise of the forecastle, letting the ship's divided worlds echo each other.
cards.student_note: This is one of the clearest Ahab-Starbuck counterpoints in the novel. It shows that resistance is morally obvious but emotionally and socially hard to carry out.

cards.title: Staged voice: First Night-Watch
cards.summary: Alone at work, Stubb turns anxiety into jokes and song. He hears enough of the captain's tension to know something is wrong, but he answers it with humor rather than panic.
cards.student_note: This chapter gives Stubb his defining tone: comic fatalism. It also shows how the crew absorbs Ahab's growing menace in small, ordinary moments.

cards.title: Staged voice: Midnight, Forecastle
cards.summary: Melville stages the crew as a crowd of voices, with sailors from many places singing, joking, and arguing. The mood turns darker when the weather changes and Pip panics at the talk of the White Whale.
cards.student_note: The chapter shows the Pequod as a mixed, global workplace, not just a backdrop for Ahab. Its theatrical form also lets Melville build tension through sound, movement, and shifting voices.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Moby Dick
cards.summary: The chapter gives Moby Dick a reputation before the whale appears in the plot. Ishmael moves through rumors, injuries, and Ahab's history until the captain's revenge seems both understandable and terrifying.
cards.student_note: This is the explanatory bridge between the oath and the hunt. It shows how fact, rumor, trauma, and symbolism merge around Moby Dick.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Whiteness of the Whale
cards.summary: Instead of explaining Moby Dick as just a dangerous animal, Ishmael studies the symbolic force of whiteness. The chapter moves through religion, race, nature, and fear to show how one color can carry contradictory meanings.
cards.student_note: This is one of the novel's major interpretive chapters. It helps students see that Moby Dick is both a whale in the plot and a symbol that resists simple decoding.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Hark!
cards.summary: Archy thinks he hears coughing and movement under the hatches, and Cabaco brushes it off as nerves and overeating. The brief exchange turns into a rumor that someone unseen is in the after-hold and that the captain knows more than he says.
cards.student_note: The chapter is all tension and no payoff, which is the point. Melville uses the whispering darkness to make the ship feel secretive and unstable before the crew fully understands what they are sailing toward.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Chart
cards.summary: Ahab's private planning becomes visible as he tracks seasons, cruising grounds, and whale movements. His obsession uses real navigational knowledge but bends it toward revenge.
cards.student_note: The chapter connects madness to method. Students can see that Ahab is dangerous partly because he is disciplined and intelligent.

cards.title: Authority and belief: The Affidavit
cards.summary: Written like a sworn statement, the chapter gathers sightings, named whales, shipwrecks, and other examples. Ishmael wants readers to see that the White Whale and the fishery's dangers are not fantasy.
cards.student_note: This is one of the novel's clearest mixes of fact and argument. It makes the story feel documented while also showing how hard it is to prove the truth of something so extreme.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Surmises
cards.summary: Ahab knows the crew still expects profit, food, routine, and whale oil. He calculates how to let the voyage function as a business while secretly steering it toward Moby Dick.
cards.student_note: This chapter clarifies the political problem of Ahab's command. He cannot simply desire; he must manage people and incentives.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Mat-Maker
cards.summary: A quiet shipboard task becomes philosophical as Ishmael imagines the mat as a loom of fate, with free will, chance, and necessity crossing one another. The stillness breaks when Tashtego spots whales and the deck suddenly snaps into motion.
cards.student_note: The chapter is classic Melville: labor becomes thought, and thought is tested by events. It also shows how quickly the novel can move from reflection to pursuit.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The First Lowering
cards.summary: The Pequod lowers boats for the first time, and the chaos of whaling becomes immediate. Fedallah and Ahab's secret crew appear, confirming that Ahab has prepared more than the crew knew.
cards.student_note: The chapter turns whaling from concept into action. It also reveals that Ahab has been concealing part of the voyage's machinery.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Hyena
cards.summary: Ishmael says there are moments when life feels like a cruel joke, and whaling is one of them. After the men are dragged back aboard, he asks about the danger, decides he may as well prepare for death, and names Queequeg as the person to handle his affairs.
cards.student_note: This chapter shows how the novel turns fear into dark comedy instead of denying it. It also makes Ishmael sound less like a heroic narrator and more like someone trying to survive panic by joking with death.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Ahab’s Boat and Crew. Fedallah
cards.summary: The narrative pauses to account for Fedallah and the private crew. Their presence makes Ahab's command feel more secretive, ritualized, and separate from normal ship order.
cards.student_note: Fedallah becomes part of the novel's prophecy and doom structure. Students should notice how Ahab surrounds himself with figures who intensify rather than restrain him.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Spirit-Spout
cards.summary: The crew repeatedly sees a ghostly whale spout, but the whale itself remains out of reach. The image turns the ocean into a space of pursuit, illusion, and omen.
cards.student_note: The chapter builds atmosphere and momentum. The hunt begins to feel guided by signs no one can fully interpret.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Albatross
cards.summary: Ishmael describes a homeward-bound whaler that looks almost ghostly from years at sea. Ahab tries to hail her about the White Whale, but the missed trumpet and crossing wakes make the meeting feel like a bad sign.
cards.student_note: The chapter turns a ship-to-ship encounter into an omen. It also widens the voyage beyond the Pequod, reminding students that other whalemen are moving through the same dangerous world.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Gam
cards.summary: Whaleships exchange hails, letters, news, and boat visits when they cross paths. Ishmael treats this as one of the most distinctive customs of the fishery, and he compares whalers to other sailors to show how unusual it is.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives students a key piece of whaling culture and a name for one of the novel's most important social rituals. It also helps explain how news and stories move across the ocean.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Town-Ho ’s Story
cards.summary: Framed as a tale told in Lima, the chapter follows the Town-Ho from its leaking hull to Radney's abuse of Steelkilt, the resulting revolt, and the later whale chase in which Moby Dick kills Radney. The story also shows how much of it stays secret, even after it is repeated on the Pequod.
cards.student_note: This is one of the novel's biggest stories within the story. It echoes Ahab's plot, makes Moby Dick's danger concrete, and shows how Melville uses oral storytelling to mix suspense, testimony, and secrecy.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Of the Monstrous Pictures of Whales
cards.summary: He moves through examples from sculpture, classical painting, Bible illustrations, and natural history books, arguing that artists usually get whales wrong because they work from guesswork or damaged bodies. The chapter keeps returning to the problem that a living whale is hard to see, let alone draw correctly.
cards.student_note: The chapter makes representation itself a classroom issue. It teaches students to question easy images and to see how difficult it is to make a believable picture of the whale.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Of the Less Erroneous Pictures of Whales, and the True Pictures of Whaling Scenes
cards.summary: He compares artists and naturalists, praising the pictures that actually catch the motion of whaling scenes. The best examples show boats, crews, spray, smoke, and the whale in action rather than as a flat specimen.
cards.student_note: This chapter continues the book's argument about how to see and represent the whale. It values lived experience and motion over neat, lifeless accuracy.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Of Whales in Paint; in Teeth; in Wood; in Sheet-Iron; in Stone; in Mountains; in Stars
cards.summary: The chapter starts with a painted sign of a whale attack, then moves through carvings on teeth and wood, metal whales on church spires, rock shapes, mountain ridges, and constellations. Whales become something the eye keeps finding, whether in the world or in the mind.
cards.student_note: This is one of the book's clearest examples of how obsession changes perception. Melville also shows that whales belong not just to biology, but to art, myth, and the way people make meaning from what they see.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Brit
cards.summary: Right whales feed in the brit like mowers moving through a field, but the calm image gives way to a harsher thought: the sea destroys ships, whales, and people without mercy. The chapter ends by contrasting that dangerous ocean with the small, fragile island of peace inside the human soul.
cards.student_note: The chapter links whale life to the book's larger philosophy of danger and vulnerability. It also shows how Melville can begin with marine ecology and end with a large claim about what it means to live in the world.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Squid
cards.summary: Daggoo sees a strange white mass rising and sinking ahead of the ship, and the men rush to the yards thinking they may have found the White Whale. Instead, they encounter a huge squid with long arms, a rare and eerie creature that few whalemen ever see and live to describe.
cards.student_note: The chapter expands the ocean beyond the whale hunt itself. It also matters for the book's natural history, because Melville uses the squid to explain what sperm whales may eat and to make the sea feel stranger than the crew expects.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Line
cards.summary: He describes the line's material, length, coiling, and the way it is laid through the boat before a hunt. What looks like simple rope becomes a system of hidden danger surrounding every crew member.
cards.student_note: The chapter turns a technical detail into a lesson about whaling's violence. It also shows one of Melville's favorite moves: making ordinary equipment feel like a reminder that life itself is precarious.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Stubb Kills a Whale
cards.summary: The chapter starts in a dreamy calm, then suddenly shifts when the whale appears under the ship. Stubb stays strangely cheerful through the chase, and the crew's quick, noisy work ends with the whale killed and the real labor of handling the body about to begin.
cards.student_note: This chapter shows Stubb's mix of humor and competence under pressure. It also reminds students that whaling can turn from boredom to violence in a few seconds.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Dart
cards.summary: The chapter describes the normal boat sequence in a whale chase: the harpooneer rows under pressure, then has to twist around and hurl the dart at the whale at exactly the right instant. Melville argues that the system exhausts the person who has to do the most dangerous work.
cards.student_note: This technical chapter makes the chase scenes easier to read and much more physical. It also reminds students that whaling is labor that pushes bodies past their limit.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Crotch
cards.summary: The crotch is the notched stick that holds the harpoon ready at the front of the boat, usually with two irons waiting. If the first iron fails, the second may have to be thrown out anyway, and once it is loose on the line it can tangle, whip around, or threaten the whole crew.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives students the gear knowledge they need for later boat scenes. It also shows how quickly whaling equipment can become a source of chaos instead of control.

cards.title: Work under pressure: Stubb’s Supper
cards.summary: The Pequod tows the dead whale in the dark, and Ahab stays restless because Moby Dick is still ahead, not behind. Stubb turns the night into a grotesque feast by having steak cut from the whale, while sharks crowd the water and strip the body below.
cards.student_note: The chapter makes whaling feel both practical and disturbing. It also shows that even a successful kill does not satisfy Ahab's larger obsession.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Whale as a Dish
cards.summary: He gives examples of whale dishes, from right-whale tongue in France to sailors dipping biscuit in whale oil, and even describes brains as food. Then he turns the disgust back on ordinary meat-eating and points out that supposedly civilized people also consume dead animals with little shame.
cards.student_note: The chapter connects appetite to violence and exposes double standards around eating. It also keeps the novel's whale obsession grounded in the body, not just in ideas.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Shark Massacre
cards.summary: Stubb sets an anchor watch so the whale can be left overnight, but the sharks gather in such numbers that Queequeg and another sailor have to attack them under lantern light. Even the dead whale is still part of a violent struggle: the crew is working to save the catch from being stripped apart before morning.
cards.student_note: The chapter keeps whale processing from feeling neat or heroic. It shows that whaling is labor shaped by damage, and it gives Queequeg one more moment of grim humor and bodily risk.

cards.title: Work under pressure: Cutting In
cards.summary: The Pequod becomes a worksite, with the mates, harpooneers, and crew all pulling together to strip the whale. The heavy body leans the ship over, and the operation feels like butchery and factory labor at the same time.
cards.student_note: This chapter explains the physical work behind whale oil. It also shows how whaling turns the ship itself into a tilted, dangerous machine.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Blanket
cards.summary: He works through the strange question of where the whale's skin really is and compares the blubber to a flexible covering that can be stripped away in pieces. The chapter mixes anatomy, analogy, and speculation instead of clean scientific certainty.
cards.student_note: Melville uses the whale's body to show how knowledge in this book is often indirect and unstable. Even a technical chapter becomes a meditation on how language reaches toward what it cannot fully name.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Funeral
cards.summary: The whale's body floats off white and huge, while sharks tear at it below and birds attack it from above. Ishmael treats the scene as a mock funeral, then widens it into a reflection on how people build dangerous beliefs from what they see.
cards.student_note: This chapter turns a dead whale into a symbol of public mourning, theft, and superstition. It also shows Melville moving from concrete shipboard labor into a bigger argument about memory, precedent, and false authority.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Sphynx
cards.summary: The detached head becomes a mute monument, and Ahab launches into one of his fiercest speeches at it. He keeps asking the head to reveal what it knows, but the only answer is silence, until the scene is interrupted by a sail in the distance.
cards.student_note: This chapter shows Ahab's habit of treating the world as if it must answer him. It also turns a whale head into a symbol of mystery that refuses to explain itself.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Jeroboam ’s Story
cards.summary: A gam with the Jeroboam brings news of Gabriel, a religiously charged figure whose influence has unsettled his ship. The encounter also connects Moby Dick to more injury and forewarning.
cards.student_note: This is one of the major warning chapters in the middle voyage. It shows that Ahab's story is not isolated; other ships have already been marked by fear, belief, and the White Whale.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Monkey-Rope
cards.summary: Ishmael has to hold Queequeg by a rope while Queequeg stands on the whale during cutting-in. The setup makes them dependent on each other, and Ishmael uses that danger to think about how every person is bound to other people in ways they cannot fully control.
cards.student_note: This is one of the novel's clearest metaphors for human interdependence. It also shows how ship labor turns friendship into something physically dangerous.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Stubb and Flask Kill a Right Whale; and Then Have a Talk Over Him
cards.summary: The Pequod drifts into a patch of right whales, and the two mates bring one alongside after a hard chase around the ship. While the whale is being secured, they trade comic talk about Ahab, Fedallah, and whether the Parsee is some kind of devilish charm on the voyage.
cards.student_note: The chapter shows ordinary shipwork continuing even when the novel pauses for whale anatomy. It also deepens the ship's atmosphere of superstition, because the crew keeps explaining events through luck, omens, and rumor.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Sperm Whale’s Head⁠—Contrasted View
cards.summary: He studies the two heads as if they were lessons in anatomy, noticing the sperm whale's dignity, the placement of its eyes and ears, and the way its massive skull differs from the right whale's shape. The chapter keeps shifting between close observation and extended comparison.
cards.student_note: Melville is not just giving facts here; he is showing how analogy and comparison shape knowledge. The chapter also prepares readers for the next one by making the contrast between the two whale types unmistakable.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Right Whale’s Head⁠—Contrasted View
cards.summary: The right whale's head looks blunt and shoe-like at first, then opens into a mouth full of baleen, or whalebone plates, that strain food from the water. Ishmael keeps spinning out comparisons, from organs and crowns to wigs and umbrellas, to make the strange anatomy legible.
cards.student_note: The chapter deepens the book's mock-scientific method and shows how much perception depends on metaphor. It also completes the contrast with the sperm whale's head so students can see the difference rather than just hear it named.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Battering-Ram
cards.summary: Melville asks the reader to look closely at the sperm whale's front end, which is a broad, boneless wall of toughness. He argues that the head is made not for delicacy but for force, and that its structure helps explain the whale's power to smash through water and objects.
cards.student_note: This is one of Melville's clearest mock-scientific arguments about the whale's anatomy. It matters because the book keeps turning natural history into a way of thinking about force, danger, and physical design.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Great Heidelburgh Tun
cards.summary: He breaks the sperm whale's head into parts, especially the junk and the case, and explains how the upper chamber holds the valuable spermaceti. The chapter treats the whale like a vast storage vessel, but it also warns how easy it is to lose or spill that precious oil while cutting it out.
cards.student_note: The chapter makes the whale's body into both a scientific object and a container of wealth. It helps students see how Melville mixes anatomy, labor, and metaphor instead of separating them.

cards.title: Work under pressure: Cistern and Buckets
cards.summary: The whale's head becomes a kind of cistern, and the crew works bucket by bucket to drain it. Then the labor goes wrong: Tashtego falls in, Daggoo and the others rush to rescue him, and the huge head nearly slips away into the sea.
cards.student_note: This chapter turns anatomy into action and danger at once. It also shows how whaling work is both absurdly technical and physically precarious.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Prairie
cards.summary: Using the language of physiognomy and phrenology, Ishmael argues that the sperm whale has no normal face to study: no nose, no readable features, only a broad forehead that looks both sublime and unknowable. He ends by admitting that human sciences cannot really decipher the whale's brow.
cards.student_note: This chapter shows how badly human categories fit the whale. It is a good example of Melville testing the limits of classification while also making fun of the systems people trust.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Nut
cards.summary: Ishmael says the sperm whale's brain is small and deeply hidden, then shifts attention to the spine and hump as possible sources of strength and character. He suggests that the whale's true power is not obvious from the outside, and that even the false brow of the skull hides what matters most.
cards.student_note: The chapter keeps pushing against simple ideas of intelligence and power. Melville keeps using anatomy to make a larger point: the most important part of a creature may be the part people cannot see at first.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Pequod Meets the Virgin
cards.summary: Derick De Deer comes aboard begging for oil because his ship is empty, then immediately throws himself into the chase when whales are sighted. The pursuit becomes a comic but brutal contest between the German boats and the Pequod, with a badly injured old bull whale trying to escape the pack.
cards.student_note: The chapter widens the voyage beyond the Pequod and shows how whaling connects ships from different nations in the same violent economy. It also mixes comedy with real danger, one of Melville's most characteristic moves.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Honor and Glory of Whaling
cards.summary: He traces whaling back through Perseus, Saint George, Hercules, Jonah, and Vishnu, arguing that the trade belongs to a long and distinguished tradition. The chapter is comic, but the argument is serious: whalers should not be treated as crude laborers when their work has deep mythic and historical roots.
cards.student_note: This chapter is Melville's direct defense of his subject. It lifts whaling into epic and religious history while also showing how Ishmael can sound both earnest and playful at the same time.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Jonah Historically Regarded
cards.summary: He collects skeptical and defensive explanations for Jonah's journey, including arguments about whale anatomy, the distance to Nineveh, and whether Jonah could have reached the city by another route. The chapter is less about proving the miracle than about showing how people argue over it.
cards.student_note: This chapter shows Melville mixing scholarship, satire, and biblical commentary. It also prepares students to read the novel's religious references as active arguments, not decorative allusions.

cards.title: Work under pressure: Pitchpoling
cards.summary: After Queequeg oils the boat to help it slide, the crew chases a running whale. When the whale is too fast to approach closely, Stubb shows the skill and nerve needed to throw the long lance from a distance and hit the animal while the boat is still in motion.
cards.student_note: The chapter turns whaling into exact, high-risk labor instead of vague adventure. It also shows how Melville can move from technical detail to comedy and violence in the same scene.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Fountain
cards.summary: He argues about how the sperm whale breathes, whether the spout is water or vapor, and why the animal must rise to the surface at regular intervals. The chapter keeps testing observation against uncertainty, then shifts into a more poetic view of the whale as a deep, misty being.
cards.student_note: This is one of the book's clearest chapters about the limits of knowledge. Ishmael is both a careful observer and a thinker who knows that even close study does not solve everything.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Tail
cards.summary: He describes the tail's size, structure, strength, and the different ways a whale uses it to move, strike, sweep, play, and dive. The chapter keeps returning to the strange mix of power and grace in the flukes, which can crush a boat or rise beautifully out of the sea.
cards.student_note: Melville turns a single body part into a lesson in form, force, and interpretation. The chapter is useful for seeing how the novel can make scientific description feel philosophical and poetic at once.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Grand Armada
cards.summary: Near the Straits of Sunda, the ship enters waters filled with whales, then has to push through a dense moving mass of spouts, bodies, and panic. The hunt becomes even more complicated when Malay pirates appear behind the Pequod, so the ship is both chasing and being chased at once.
cards.student_note: This chapter enlarges the scale of the voyage and shows the world of whaling as global, crowded, and unstable. It also gives students one of Melville's most vivid scenes of herd behavior, pursuit, and confusion.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Schools and Schoolmasters
cards.summary: Schools and Schoolmasters turns whale behavior into a social lesson about age, sex, grouping, and solitude.
cards.student_note: The chapter deepens cetology by showing Ishmael using animal life to think through human social language and its limits.

cards.title: Rules and power: Fast-Fish and Loose-Fish
cards.summary: Ishmael explains the difference between a Fast-Fish, already claimed, and a Loose-Fish, available to whoever can seize it. He then applies the rule far beyond whaling.
cards.student_note: This chapter shows Melville's habit of turning technical details into political thought. It is especially useful for discussions of ownership, colonialism, and legal language.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Heads or Tails
cards.summary: He uses a legal case to show how a whale, once claimed under royal authority, could be treated as property for the crown. Then he follows the logic through old law books and jokes about why the king gets the head and the queen gets the tail.
cards.student_note: The chapter turns law into a study of power and ownership. It is especially useful for discussing how Melville links whaling to monarchy, property, and the strange language of authority.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Pequod Meets the Rose-Bud
cards.summary: Stubb approaches a French ship carrying two bad catches: one blasted whale and one dried-out carcass that still might contain ambergris. He jokes with the captain and crew, then pulls a small haul of the fragrant substance from the whale's body.
cards.student_note: The chapter mixes comedy, commerce, and decay. It shows how even a horrible-smelling whale can still be valuable, and it introduces ambergris as one of the book's strangest whale products.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Ambergris
cards.summary: He defines ambergris, notes that it is prized in perfume and sometimes cooking, and traces the bad smell associated with whaling to older methods of handling whale oil. The chapter ends by insisting that whales are not inherently filthy and can even seem fragrant.
cards.student_note: This digression matters because Melville keeps turning the whale into a scientific and commercial object. It also shows how the novel can move from slapstick smell to a serious argument about value and perception.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Castaway
cards.summary: During a whale chase, Pip panics, jumps from the boat, and is left alone in the vast ocean before being recovered. The trauma changes him, and the ship treats that change uneasily.
cards.student_note: Pip becomes one of the novel's most important late figures. The chapter also forces students to face the human cost of shipboard discipline and whaling danger.

cards.title: Work under pressure: A Squeeze of the Hand
cards.summary: The work of pressing the whale oil back into fluid becomes oddly soothing, even affectionate. Ishmael describes losing anger and feeling as if the whole crew should be drawn together in shared softness, before moving on to more practical details of whale processing.
cards.student_note: This chapter matters because it makes physical labor shape emotion and thought. It also shows Melville's habit of finding tenderness inside industrial work without pretending the work is harmless.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Cassock
cards.summary: Melville describes the long strip of whale skin pulled over the mincer's body, then shows him standing at the wooden horse where blubber is chopped into pieces for the pots. The result makes shipboard butchery look like a strange religious office.
cards.student_note: The chapter is a sharp example of Melville turning labor into ritual imagery. It also shows how the whale gets converted from body to commodity through very physical work.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Try-Works
cards.summary: The try-works make the Pequod into a floating factory at night. Ishmael describes the heat, smoke, and danger until the ship seems almost hellish.
cards.student_note: This chapter is essential for understanding whaling as industrial labor. It also gives one of the strongest symbolic images of the voyage consuming itself.

cards.title: Work under pressure: Stowing Down and Clearing Up
cards.summary: Melville walks through the process of stowing the oil below deck and scrubbing away the blood, soot, and blubber above. The ship becomes clean enough to seem domestic, but the next cry for a whale will start the cycle again.
cards.student_note: This chapter captures the grind of whale work better than any single chase scene. It also shows the novel's larger pattern: labor, cleanup, and renewed danger repeat before anyone gets much rest.

cards.title: Staged voice: The Doubloon
cards.summary: The doubloon becomes a shared object that each major character reads according to his own desire or fear.
cards.student_note: The chapter is a compact lesson in interpretation: one coin produces many meanings because each reader brings a different pressure to it.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Leg and Arm
cards.summary: The two wounded captains compare injuries, and the English captain tells the story of losing his arm in a whale attack. His surgeon, Dr. Bunger, joins the conversation, and Ahab listens closely for any clue about the White Whale's last movements before rushing back to his own ship.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives Ahab another version of obsession's cost in the body. It also deepens the novel's network of ship encounters, where other lives and injuries reflect his own hunt.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Decanter
cards.summary: He explains how the Enderbys helped open new whale grounds, then recalls a convivial night aboard the Samuel Enderby with flip, beef, bread, and plenty of noise. From there he turns to old Dutch records to show how richly whalers were provisioned.
cards.student_note: The chapter places whaling inside a larger history of trade, national rivalry, and shipboard abundance. It also gives one of the book's clearest comic contrasts between grim labor and generous eating and drinking.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: A Bower in the Arsacides
cards.summary: He says he learned the whale's bones from a stranded skeleton in the Arsacides, where the priests treat it like a sacred object. The chapter is partly travel memory, partly anatomy lesson, and partly an argument that the whale has to be understood in more than one piece at a time.
cards.student_note: This chapter pushes the book deeper into whale anatomy and display. It also turns a skeleton into a scene of wonder, helping students see how Melville mixes science, storytelling, and reverence.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Measurement of the Whale’s Skeleton
cards.summary: He compares the Tranque whale's length, ribs, spine, and skull to the larger body it once held, then points out how much flesh, weight, and force are missing from the bones. He also treats the skeleton as something that can be checked against other museum specimens, not just his own memory.
cards.student_note: The chapter teaches students how Melville can sound scientific while still insisting on uncertainty. It also keeps the whale bigger than any single method of description.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Fossil Whale
cards.summary: He gathers examples of ancient whale bones, compares them with modern whale anatomy, and describes a huge extinct whale found in Alabama that was first misread as a reptile. He ends by linking whales to old monuments, temples, and prehuman history.
cards.student_note: This chapter makes the whale feel ancient enough to outlast ordinary human history. It is also a strong example of Melville turning natural history into a meditation on scale, extinction, and wonder.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish?⁠—Will He Perish?
cards.summary: He weighs old claims about gigantic whales against newer measurements, then takes up the harder question of whether relentless hunting could wipe the whale out. By the end, he still believes the species is strong enough, widespread enough, and long-lived enough to survive human pressure.
cards.student_note: The chapter turns whale biology into a debate about extinction and human damage. It gives students an early example of Melville thinking about species survival on a huge scale.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Ahab’s Leg
cards.summary: Ahab's artificial leg has been strained by the voyage, and its repair draws attention to his injury. His body is both a source of vulnerability and a sign of the revenge story he cannot release.
cards.student_note: The chapter keeps Ahab embodied rather than abstract. His obsession is tied to pain, disability, command, and pride.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Carpenter
cards.summary: The carpenter works at his vice-bench repairing, shaping, drilling, and improvising across many kinds of shipboard tasks. He seems emotionally detached and almost machine-like, but he is still a living, comic presence who can keep the ship functioning in constant emergencies.
cards.student_note: This chapter shows labor as intelligence, not just background work. The carpenter also becomes an important contrast to Ahab: practical, impersonal, and useful in a world the captain keeps trying to bend to his will.

cards.title: Work under pressure: Ahab and the Carpenter
cards.summary: As the carpenter files and measures the ivory leg, Ahab jokes, philosophizes, and grows restless with the fact that he needs another man's work to stand. He talks about the strange feeling of a lost limb, the limits of the body, and the uneasy idea that a person can still be haunted by what is no longer there.
cards.student_note: This chapter keeps Ahab physical, vulnerable, and unsettlingly self-aware. It also links his private injury to the larger themes of debt, control, and the body's stubborn presence in the novel.

cards.title: Staged voice: Ahab and Starbuck in the Cabin
cards.summary: When the ship's oil casks leak, Starbuck argues that the crew must stop and repair them. Ahab resists because practical duty competes with the hunt, and the confrontation becomes dangerously personal.
cards.student_note: This is a key Ahab-Starbuck conflict. It shows that the ship's economic purpose and moral order still have defenders, but Ahab can overpower them.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Queequeg in His Coffin
cards.summary: Queequeg believes he is near death and has a coffin made according to his wishes. When he decides to live, the coffin remains aboard as an object waiting for a new meaning.
cards.student_note: The chapter prepares the epilogue without announcing it. It also returns emotional attention to Queequeg after many chapters of shipboard systems and Ahab's will.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Pacific
