cards.summary: Ishmael describes the Pacific as a vast, mysterious sea that seems alive with memory and dreaming. Ahab, however, does not relax into the beauty; he stays fixed on the White Whale as the ship moves toward the Japanese cruising grounds.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives the voyage a change of atmosphere and a wider world. It also shows how beauty and obsession can exist side by side without canceling each other out.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Blacksmith
cards.summary: While he keeps repairing weapons and tools on deck, Ishmael tells the story of how drink, loss, and poverty broke his family and his body. Perth's life ashore ended in ruin, so the sea becomes a place to keep working and keep going.
cards.student_note: This chapter gives one of the book's clearest portraits of damaged labor. It also shows that whaling attracts men who are running from grief as much as men chasing profit.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Forge
cards.summary: Ahab gathers materials and crew energy to make a weapon for the final hunt. The scene feels like a dark ceremony, with practical craft transformed into symbolic violence.
cards.student_note: This chapter makes Ahab's obsession tangible. The harpoon becomes a physical extension of his will.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Gilder
cards.summary: The Pequod works through long stretches of quiet weather, and the ocean looks almost gentle and pastoral. Ishmael notes that the peace does not last, but it briefly changes how the crew sees the world, especially when Starbuck and Stubb speak from within the glow of the sea.
cards.student_note: The chapter balances the novel's violence with a moment of beauty. It also shows how Melville lets different characters answer the same scene in very different ways.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Pequod Meets the Bachelor
cards.summary: The Bachelor is homeward-bound, richly loaded, and almost festive, with a captain who does not believe in the White Whale. Ahab asks after Moby Dick, gets no useful answer, and moves on without sharing the other ship's joy.
cards.student_note: This encounter sharpens the contrast between plenty and obsession, celebration and doom. It also shows Ahab becoming more isolated from the ordinary life of the sea.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Dying Whale
cards.summary: After a successful hunt, Ahab sees the whale turn its head toward the sun as it dies, then reflects on how life seems to seek light even at the end. The sight pushes him into a dark, philosophical speech about death, the sea, and the forces that rule both.
cards.student_note: The chapter gives Ahab one of his more reflective moments, but it does not soften him. It helps students see how he keeps translating natural events into a private metaphysics.

cards.title: Whale knowledge: The Whale Watch
cards.summary: Fedallah's prophecy seems to promise safety while actually tightening the terms of Ahab's doom.
cards.student_note: The riddles matter because they return during the chase. Ahab hears them through desire, not caution.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Quadrant
cards.summary: The quadrant can help locate the ship, but it cannot locate Moby Dick or justify Ahab's purpose. Ahab smashes it and chooses his own will over a tool of orientation.
cards.student_note: The chapter dramatizes Ahab's rejection of limits. He refuses a world that can guide position but not meaning.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Candles
cards.summary: The Pequod enters a terrifying electrical storm. While the crew is frightened, Ahab addresses the fire as if it confirms rather than rebukes him.
cards.student_note: This chapter raises the voyage to apocalyptic intensity. It also contrasts natural terror with Ahab's refusal to submit.

cards.title: Staged voice: Midnight.⁠—The Forecastle Bulwarks
cards.summary: The two mates argue about risk, insurance, lightning, and whether Ahab's ship is really any more dangerous than others. Their talk keeps moving between common sense and absurdity, which fits a ship trying to hold itself together in bad weather.
cards.student_note: This chapter shows how sailors manage fear with wit and technical talk. It also keeps the storm sequence human, noisy, and grounded in ordinary labor.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Musket
cards.summary: With Ahab asleep and a loaded musket nearby, Starbuck sees a possible way to save the ship. He thinks through the moral terror of murder, obedience, and responsibility before stepping back.
cards.student_note: This is Starbuck's sharpest crisis. It shows how hard it is to resist Ahab even when resistance may be the only way to save lives.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Needle
cards.summary: The storm has damaged the Pequod's compass, but Ahab turns the failure into another display of mastery. He creates a new needle and restores direction by force of will.
cards.student_note: The chapter shows why the crew keeps following him: Ahab can still solve real problems. His competence makes his madness more dangerous.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Log and Line
cards.summary: A practical attempt to measure the ship's speed breaks down. Pip's presence turns the moment inward, pressing Ahab toward truths he does not want to face.
cards.student_note: Navigation tools keep failing as the ending nears. Pip becomes a human counter-instrument, measuring moral and spiritual damage.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Life-Buoy
cards.summary: After a death at sea exposes the need for a new life-buoy, Queequeg's coffin is repurposed. The object now carries both burial and rescue in the same form.
cards.student_note: This chapter prepares Ishmael's survival. It also shows Melville's habit of making objects change meaning under pressure.

cards.title: Work under pressure: The Deck
cards.summary: The carpenter treats the coffin-life-buoy as a job; Ahab cannot help reading it as a sign. Their exchange makes the ship feel full of objects that are both useful and ominous.
cards.student_note: The chapter keeps symbolic pressure high without leaving shipboard labor behind. Practical work and metaphysical dread occupy the same deck.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Pequod Meets the Rachel
cards.summary: Captain Gardiner of the Rachel pleads for help finding lost whaleboats, including one carrying his son. Ahab rejects the appeal and continues the hunt.
cards.student_note: This is one of Ahab's clearest moral failures. He chooses revenge over another captain's children, making the cost of obsession painfully concrete.

cards.title: Staged voice: The Cabin
cards.summary: Pip's attachment to Ahab creates a moment of tenderness and danger. Ahab recognizes that staying near Pip might call him back toward human feeling.
cards.student_note: The chapter shows Ahab resisting compassion because it threatens the hunt. It also deepens Pip's late role as a witness to damage.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Hat
cards.summary: The chapter focuses on Ahab's exposed figure and the ominous theft of his hat by a sea bird. The ship is close to its goal, and even small events feel charged.
cards.student_note: The final atmosphere tightens here. Ahab's authority remains visible, but the world around him seems to be stripping it away.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Pequod Meets the Delight
cards.summary: Another ship has encountered the White Whale and suffered for it. Instead of turning back, Ahab reads the damage as proof that he is close.
cards.student_note: This is the last major warning before the chase. It shows the closed loop of Ahab's mind: every sign against the hunt becomes a sign to continue.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Symphony
cards.summary: Near the end, Ahab speaks with unusual tenderness about age, family, and the cost of his obsession. Starbuck nearly reaches him, but Ahab remains trapped by the purpose he has made for himself.
cards.student_note: This is the strongest late challenge to Ahab's course. It makes the ending feel tragic rather than merely inevitable.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Chase⁠—First Day
cards.summary: Ahab and the crew sight the white whale and lower boats in pursuit. The first day confirms both Moby Dick's physical power and Ahab's refusal to stop.
cards.student_note: The final action begins here. The chapter turns long-building obsession into direct confrontation.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Chase⁠—Second Day
cards.summary: Moby Dick again defeats the hunters, and the signs around Ahab grow darker. Instead of retreating, he treats injury and warning as proof that the chase must go on.
cards.student_note: The chapter shows the logic of obsession at its most dangerous: every reason to stop becomes fuel for continuing.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: The Chase⁠—Third Day
cards.summary: On the third day, Ahab strikes at Moby Dick and is caught in the fatal consequences of his pursuit. The Pequod sinks, and the crew is swallowed by the disaster their captain forced upon them.
cards.student_note: This is the novel's catastrophe. It completes the revenge plot while leaving Ishmael's survival for the epilogue to explain.

cards.title: Scene and judgment: Epilogue
cards.summary: The epilogue explains how Ishmael lived to tell the story. Queequeg's coffin becomes a life-buoy, and the Rachel, still searching for its own lost children, finds Ishmael instead.
cards.student_note: Without the epilogue, the narration has no survivor. The ending also turns an object associated with death into the means of preserving the story.
